title: `new Worker()` should automatically gain `worker` target environment (vis-a-vis package.json exports)
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
When running `new Worker()`, all the imports in that entry-point should use `worker` [target environment](https://webpack.js.org/guides/package-exports/#target-environment)

Instead, it seems the default is just browser.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
Makes it easier to build libraries that are compatible with workers (i.e. same motivation as browser/node conditional exports)


**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
As stated in title.


**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
No
author: nemosmithasf

title: Is it possible to have deterministic build output?
body:
Hello,

Every time I run our build, if I look at the size of the output chunk files, it is different every single time.  Usually the number of chunk files is the same, but the size on disk of the files is different.  None of the source code or settings have changed in between builds.  This is just running the same build multiple times.

Here are the total chunk sizes from 8 runs of our build, one after another, in bytes:

- 200011996
- 200027705
- 200011666
- 200020535
- 200016600
- 200020336
- 200050837

The difference between the biggest and smallest is 39171 bytes.  Percentage-wise this is totally insignificant, like 0.02% of the total build size.  So it's not a problem for the build itself.

The problem is that we are trying to implement build checks that detect when somebody has made a change that is causing the build output to increase.  And with non-deterministic output, it makes it difficult to pin down a specific change to blame, so we can catch it before it gets merged.  A commit that increases the size, might make it under the current threshold, and then that code could accidentally fail the check in somebody else's PR because they happen to be unlucky and hit a build that comes in on the high side.

We could implement our check based on module sizes, but that doesn't take into consideration the duplication that can happen during chunk splitting.  We're hoping to have out check be done on the true final output size of the build.

Any ideas about why this non-deterministic output might be happening?   I'm guessing it's during splitChunks, but I don't know for sure.

title: ResolverCachePlugin: Allow dynamic multiple time resolve of the same path
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request
In Webpack 4, resolver resolve path multiple time (no cache), therefor plugin like this works: https://www.npmjs.com/package/dynamic-resolve-webpack-plugin. But in Webpack 5, ResolverCachePlugin automatically snapshot a resolve, this can't be modified without affecting other resolve.

Therefor, I am requesting ResolverCachePlugin adding condition whether the file need to be resolved again or not. The condition might either based on webpack option or plugin signal to determine.

**What is the expected behavior?**

```
{
        resolve: {
            plugins: [
                new DynamicAliasResolvePlugin({
                    alias: "@template",
                    dynamic: (request, alias) => {
                        return request.context.issuer.replace(/\.js$/, '/template/');
                    },
                }),
            ]
        },
}
```

Using it like these on multiple files:

page-a.js
```
// Resolve to "./page-a/template/"
import Template from "@template/homepage";
```

page-b.js
```
// Resolve to "./page-b/template/"
import Template from "@template/homepage";
```

Two of the paths will be resolved on different path and not cached. 

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
The main motivation is the use of plugin such as DynamicAliasResolvePlugin. Currently, only `page-a.js` get resolved and `page-b.js` will use the previous resolve which led it to be unusable.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
There are two way to implements this,

1. Add more options to `snapshot.resolve` that will be used to determine whether a file path need to be cached or not.
2. Pass and get config to resolver plugin whether the resulting resolve should be snapshot or not.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes, with guidance from the maintainers.

title: Concern for running Multiple Angular Micro-frontends in Angular App Shell
body:
We want to have multiple versions of Angular [micro-frontends](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microfrontend#:%7E:text=Microfrontend%20is%20a%20front%2Dend,for%20multiple%20front%2Dend%20applications) running within an Angular [app shell](https://angular.io/guide/app-shell#:%7E:text=Application%20shell%20is%20a%20way,automatically%20after%20the%20code%20loads). Is module federation using webpack5 currently the best approach? Looking towards future iterations of Angular, such as when Angular Signals are released, are there alternative methods that we should consider? Additionally, when webpack6 is released, it may not be backwards compatible, so we may need to continue using Webpack5. Would this cause any issues?

For example: The Angular Host app is running Angular 15 with one child Angular micro frontend running Angular 14 and one child Angular micro front end running Angular 13. We will need both Angular 14 and 13 micro frontends to communicate with each other and with the Angular 15 host.

title: Webpack6 module federation and backwards compatibility
body: Currently we're using module federation with webpack to run multiple versions of Angular micro-frontends in our Angular app shell. When webpack6 comes out, we're wondering if it'll still be possible to support two different versions of Angular and if it'll be backward compatible with webpack5.

<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
When using the `background-image` CSS property with webpack, and the path to some asset is given like `background-image: url('any-img.png')`, webpack should first try to resolve it from `src`, but if not found, webpack should consider it as a public asset which would be served from the public folder.


**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
Suppose we've configured a web app with webpack, and we've a nicely working public folder, and all public folder assets are directly served at `/` (root) of the application. But when I try to use the public assets as `background-image` in CSS (eg: `background-image: url('/assets/bg.png')`) it doesn't work, because it just tries to resolve it in `src`. I think it is clearly mentioned in the syntax (`background-image: url('/assets/bg.png')`) that path given is a URL, but still, webpack tries to load it from src, instead of considering it a URL.


**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

Note: this might be an issue with my webpack implementation, kindly suggest me some resources if it is already possible with webpack. Thanks!


**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes

author: TheLarkInn

title: Import, unreachable after applying the values in `DefinePlugin`, appears in built product anyway
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

Observe that when `__BROWSER__` is made `true` through the use of `DefinePlugin`, this code:

```ts
import nodeFetchImpl from "node-fetch";

let fetchImpl;
if (__BROWSER__) {
  fetchImpl = globalThis.fetch;
} else {
  fetchImpl = nodeFetchImpl;
}

export default fetchImpl;
```

…gets transformed to this:

```js
import*as e from"node-fetch";var r={544:r=>{r.exports=e}},t={};function o(e){var a=t[e];if(void 0!==a)return a.exports;var s=t[e]={exports:{}};return r[e](s,s.exports,o),s.exports}var a,s,p={};s=p,Object.defineProperty(s,"X",{value:!0}),o(544),a=globalThis.fetch,s.Z=a;var f=p.X,v=p.Z;export{f as __esModule,v as default};
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

Repro: https://github.com/steveluscher/webpack-dce-repro

**What is the expected behavior?**

Since:

1. `node-fetch` has `"sideEffects": false` in its `package.json`, and
2. The `node-fetch` import is unreachable when `__BROWSER__` is `true`

I would expect the compiled output not to contain `import*as e from"node-fetch";` or any of the code associated with `e`.

**Other relevant information:**

```
Binaries:
  Node: 16.20.0 - /usr/local/bin/node
  npm: 8.19.4 - /usr/local/bin/npm
Packages:
  ts-loader: ^9.4.2 => 9.4.2 
  webpack: ^5.79.0 => 5.79.0 
  webpack-cli: ^5.0.1 => 5.0.1 
```

title: Hot Reloading Issue
body:
I have a `dist/index.html` file that refers to a bundled JS script. However, hot reloading only works when this script is in the same directory as `index.html` but fails when I place the script in a subdirectory (`dist/assets`). Check out my entire project structure:

```
webpack-test
│   .babelrc
│   webpack.config.js
│   package-lock.json
│   package.json
│
├───dist
│   │   index.html
│   │   bundle.js
│   │
│   └───assets
│           bundle.js
│
└───src
        index.js
```

Here is my webpack config:

```
const path = require('path');

module.exports = {
    entry: './src/index.js',
    output: {
        path: path.resolve(__dirname, 'dist/assets'),
        filename: 'bundle.js'
    },
    devServer: {
        port: 9000,
        compress: true,
        hot: true,
        static: {
            directory: path.join(__dirname, 'dist')
        }
    }
}
```

So when I indicate `<script src="./index.js"></script>` in `dist/index.html` hot reloading works as expected. It doesn't work when I do `<script src="./assets/index.js"></script>` though. I suppose the config is correct but I'm not sure.
author: ghost

title: what's the proper way to use non-trivial web workers across domains?
body:
Apologies if this has been answered before, but I have only been able to find pieces of solutions here and there.

I have a web application that contains a standalone react component bundled using webpack. This component spawns web workers, and these workers load and execute WebAssembly code, which is all included in the bundle. Everything works fine if the application and the react component are hosted on the same server.

I now have a need to serve the react component from a CDN, splitting it from the main application's host. The worker was originally created using the syntax that allows webpack to statically identify and bundle the worker script:

```
new Worker(new URL('../workers/my.worker.ts', import.meta.url))
```

Suppose the main app is served from app.com and the component is served from cdn.com. The bundle on cdn.com is accessible and its entry point loads fine from app.com, but when `new Worker()` is called, it causes this error:

```
DOMException: Failed to construct 'Worker': Script at 'https://cdn.com/component/component.647.chunk.js' cannot be accessed from origin 'https://app.com'
```

I could not find a way to make this work across domains, even with `Content-Security-Policy: worker-src` headers. So I implemented [this workaround](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions/14648#discussioncomment-1589272) from @piotr-oles, which uses `importScripts` inside a blob URL and aliases `Worker` to preserve the special webpack syntax. With this change, the worker is accessible and loads partially, but then I see errors like this:

```
DOMException: Failed to execute 'importScripts' on 'WorkerGlobalScope': The script at 'https://cdn.com/component/component.647.chunk.js' failed to load.
```

Tracking down the cause of this reveals that the worker script itself is trying to load another bundled chunk, since the worker code contains its own imports, but it is requesting the wrong URL: it's trying to load `blob:https://app.com/component.402.chunk.js` when it should be trying to load `https://cdn.com/component/component.402.chunk.js`. It seems that webpack's internal `importScripts` call is using the wrong prefix - that of the temporary blob URL from the workaround above - to try to load other bundled resources from the worker.

So, I'm stumped with several questions.
1. Is it impossible to just create the worker directly using the `new Worker()` syntax, even if I have control of both app.com and cdn.com?
2. If so, is the referenced blob URL method the recommended workaround, even if the worker script calls `import` / needs to load other chunks?
3. Perhaps I have the wrong approach altogether - what is missing here? Do I need to set `publicPath` and/or the new `workerPublicPath` in the webpack config, or use different http headers, or change something else in the config or code...?

Any guidance is appreciated!

title: less-loader: can warnings become errors?
body:
I would like warnings from `less-loader` to be configurable to become webpack errors.

A typical mistake I see our team make is this, which comes up as a warning:

```
LOG from ./node_modules/less-loader/dist/cjs.js less-loader ./node_modules/css-loader/dist/cjs.js??ruleSet[1].rules[6].use[1]!./node_modules/less-loader/dist/cjs.js!./src/main/js/views/visual.module.less
<w> extend ' .body1' has no matches
```

And this happens because someone wrote

```
        div {
            &:extend(.body1);
        }
```

But forgot to import our typography module

```
@import "~lib/system/typography.module.less";
```

Unfortunately, it's very easy to miss this warning, and I would like it to be configurable as an error. I have looked in the `less-loader` documentation but I didn't find anything. Using `WarningsToErrorsPlugin` doesn't work for this case since it's just a log?

I think my real question is, is this a feature request for `less-loader`?

title: Worker type=module
body:
I'm trying to understand how module workers should work with Webpack. 

Let's start with the example I found in the repo: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/main/examples/worker/README.md

Looking at this example, there's a `new Worker(..., { type: "module" })` [link](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blame/0e0dab0a4b75bf84fa4d9a974e6cc55f70486352/examples/worker/README.md#L85) in the source file but the compiled output has `new Worker(..., { type: undefined })` [link](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blame/0e0dab0a4b75bf84fa4d9a974e6cc55f70486352/examples/worker/README.md#L482).

I'm trying to understand how to change the output behavior that emits the synchronous `importScripts()` instead of an async ESM `import()`.

For reference, I was basing my understanding of ESM in Workers and preloading based on this article: https://web.dev/module-workers/.

title: Tree shaking eliminates only part of package's code
body:
Hi

I have the following scenario: I created a package of react components (which uses mui and the styled utility exported from @mui/material/styles). I bundle this package with rollup. Then, I'm using the package in an app which I bundle with webpack.
The package.json of the package includes **sideEffects: false**

In the app, I use only one of the two exported components I have in the package. The component that I **don't** use (called TextWithIcon) is comprised of a .tsx file that includes the react component, and a .styles.tsx file that includes the styled object. The .tsx file imports the .styles.tsx file.

When I build the app in production mode, I notice that the bundle **excludes** (as expected) the code of the actual component (the code in the .tsx file) but **includes** the code in the styled object (the code in the .styles.tsx file) although this code is never used.

So I wonder

1. Why does the .styles.tsx code remain in the webpack bundle if I don't use it?
2. I tried to avoid using rollup, and just transpiled the code of the package with tsc, and then the unused code was indeed eliminated. So why with un-bundled code webpack knows to eliminate the unused code?

App code: [here](https://github.com/yishayweb/app-example)
Package bundled with rollup code: [here](https://github.com/yishayweb/package-example)

Would really appreciate if someone can clarify this

Thanks !!
author: yishayweb

author: klynsaha

title: pathToFileURL is not a function with Web Worker in Webpack 5
body:
Hi,

Attempting to follow instructions from the documentation site to have a Web Worker with Webpack 5 (in a .ts file, using babel):

```
const worker = new Worker(new URL('./file.worker.js', import.meta.url))
```

latest version 5.81.0 yields an exception `webpack_require__(...).pathToFileURL is not a function` 

not sure if related to Webpack 5 not exporting built-in node module `url`  anymore, I didn't understand what's the way to make this work.

I see some browser polyfills for `url` module but which don't have the function `pathToFileURL`

It seems Webpack didn't convert this code correctly:


If using `resolve.fallback.url: false`:

```
const worker = new Worker(new URL('./file.worker.js', (__webpack_require__(/*! url */ "?06cf").pathToFileURL)(__filename).toString()));
```

else if the `url` module is available (from https://github.com/defunctzombie/node-url):

```
const worker = new Worker(new URL('./EventBuffer.worker.js', __webpack_require__(/*! url */ "../../node_modules/url/url.js").pathToFileURL(__filename).toString()));
```


Any suggestions, known hints?

Thanks,

title: How do I import an ES module into my application using a native import map
body:
I have an application being built using Webpack. I want to import a remote ES module using a native import map that's defined in my HTML page. From my application code, I'd like to import this ES module using an import statement:

```
import woof from "woof";
```

I do not want to do this:

```
const woof = async import('woof');
```

Is this possible? If so, how?

From reading the docs on `externalsType.module`, I see:

> Specify the default type of externals as 'module'. Webpack will generate code like import * as X from '...' for externals used in a module.

The term _module_ is generally vague (ES module specifically? Any type of module?), so I'm not sure if it applies to my situation. I also note that it says:

> Make sure to enable [experiments.outputModule](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/experiments/#experimentsoutputmodule) first, otherwise webpack will throw errors.

I'm not really trying to output my application as a module (I don't think so anyway), so this makes me feel like this is _not_ what I need.

Here's a repository that reflects basically what I'm trying to accomplish: https://github.com/Aaronius/webpack-import-map

Thanks for your help.

title: Restrict dynamic require via config
body:
👋 One of the dependencies that I use in a project has a dynamic require statement in it:

```js
...
const foo = require(`some/path/${bar}`);
...
```

Is there a way from my Webpack config to restrict what this dynamic require can load? By default it's causing a massive chunk to be created with every possible file under `some/path`, but I'd like to restrict it to a small list of files that I will actually be needing.

Again, this is a dependency, so I can't really use magic comments or similar to do this in the source code itself, I'd like to do this via my Webpack config.

title: [Bug] using Module Federation with an additional Entrypoint prevents availability of init and get
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->


**What is the current behavior?**

i am using module federation with two angular 15 projects.

now i wanted to add an additional entrypoint script ( like described in your [docs ](https://webpack.js.org/concepts/module-federation/#infer-publicpath-from-script)), but doing this creates the issue that the init and get functions are not available anymore

![image](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/40056245/f4910bf0-c825-45ea-a074-bbd845b1dc5b)



**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

using a webpack config like this works:

`module.exports = {
    output: {
        publicPath: "http://localhost:4201/",
        uniqueName: "appAnalyzer",
        scriptType: "text/javascript"
    },
    optimization: {
        runtimeChunk: false
    },
    plugins: [
        new ModuleFederationPlugin({
            name: "appAnalyzer",
            filename: "remoteEntry.js",
            exposes: {
                ...
            },
        })
    ]
};`

using a webpack.config with an additional entrypoint creates the issue:

`module.exports = {
    entry: {
        main: "./app/main.ts"
        appAnalyzer: "./app/setPublicPath.js" // same with  "./app/main.ts"
    },
    output: {
        publicPath: "http://localhost:4201/",
        uniqueName: "appAnalyzer",
        scriptType: "text/javascript"
    },
    optimization: {
        runtimeChunk: false
    },
    plugins: [
        new ModuleFederationPlugin({
            name: "appAnalyzer",
            filename: "remoteEntry.js",
            exposes: {
                ...
            },
        })
    ]
};`

**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

the get and init Methods should still be available 


i debugged the generated "remoteEntry.js" files and noticed that here is a difference 

![image](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/40056245/98249055-eacb-4298-9694-c4d7819804d6)

i created a small sciprt in my frame appliction and
switching 12196 with 34731 in the loaded files resolves the issue.
(with 34731being the entrypoint script and 12196 the container entry script)

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.81
Node.js version: 16.16.0

my package.json contains

'
"webpack": "5.81.x",
"@angular/common": "15.2.8",
"@angular/core": "15.2.8",
"@angular/cdk": "15.2.8",
"@angular/cli": "15.2.7",
'

Thanks in Advance
author: MariusZeger

title: DevServer: how to specify a different content root?
body:
Scenario: 

I have 3 DevServer instances running on localhost:8081, localhost:8082, and localhost:8083. They all use ModuleFederation and serve an entrypoint called myEntry1.js, myEntry2.js etc.

Further, I have proxy server running at localhost:9090. It is supposed to delegate calls to one of the DevServer instances based on some internal logic.

Now, if I request for example localhost:9090/myEntry1.js, the proxy will retrieve the corresponding file from localhost:8081 just fine.

HOWEVER, when myEntry1.js in turn starts loading chunks, it does so by making calls DIRECTLY to localhost:8081, bypassing the proxy.

Is there any way to override this behavior, either to specify a specific origin, or to simply use the origin of the original request (e.g. localhost:9090 in this example)?
author: csvan

title: Webpack `cache.buildDependencies` dependencies resolver works incorectly.
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the webpack Gitter. -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- https://gitter.im/webpack/webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->


My `webpack congig` depends on `package-a`.

`package-a` is depends on `package-b`;

For development purposes packages `package-a` and `package-b` symlinked to the `node_modules`.

I set `NODE_PRESERVE_SYMLINKS=1`, so nodejs will not resolves symlinks.

I added my `webpack congig` location to the `cache.buildDependencies`.

Whe I am running build process I got error:

`package-b` can't be resolved from the `package-a`.
And in error message it shows original `package-a` location (not symlink location).

So, seems, that when webpack resolves `cache.buildDependencies` dependencies it does not respect `NODE_PRESERVE_SYMLINKS=1` and follwos symbolic links.

This disables filesysten cache.

**What is the expected behavior?**

I am expecting, that when `NODE_PRESERVE_SYMLINKS=1` is set - modules resilver will not follw symbolic links. As it implemented in the nodejs.

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.82.0
Node.js version: 20.0.1
Operating System: win / linux
Additional tools: none
author: zdm

title: v5.82.1
body:
## Bug Fixes * [CSS] - Support nesting in CSS modules and bug fixes by @alexander-akait in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/17133 * [CSS] - Fix crash with `importModule` when CSS enabled by @alexander-akait in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/17140 * Fix bug where `output.hashFunction` was failing to generate debug hash by @ahabhgk in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/16950 * Reduce the amount of generated code for chunk loading by @lvivski in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/17151 * Use module preload for ESM module output by @alexander-akait in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/17143 ## Developer Experience * Improve module type strictness for Module.prototype.type expand ModuleTypeConstants by @TheLarkInn in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/17136 ## Dependencies & Maintenance * Update package.json description by @JeraldVin in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/17145 * Bump webpack-cli from 5.0.2 to 5.1.0 by @dependabot in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/17146 * Bump core-js from 3.30.1 to 3.30.2 by @dependabot in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/17149 * Bump enhanced-resolve to v5.14.0 by @snitin315 in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/17160 ## New Contributors * @JeraldVin made their first contribution in https://github.com/webpack/webpack/pull/17145 **Full Changelog**: https://github.com/webpack/webpack/compare/v5.82.0...v5.82.1
This discussion was created from the release v5.82.1.
author: TheLarkInn

title: Env variables shouldn't get revealed in the bundled js file
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request
Make webpack compiled env variables end-user safe

**What is the expected behavior?**
Currently, any process.env variable gets shown "as it is" in the compiled bundled.js.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
This clearly defeats the purpose of having environmental variables, because anyone can see the data by analyzing the js file.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
Instead of being added to the bundled.js file, an env file should be generated at runtime called bundled.env, also to be placed in the dist folder, and in such a way that end users aren't able to access the values to the env variables.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
it's beyond my paygrade, sorry
author: harshnambiar

title: Understanding Webpack cache contents
body:
👋 We've been running into a pretty consistent issue on Vercel recently where our Next.js cache is over 1.00GB, and, frankly, I have no idea why it is that large. It appears to be mostly due to the Webpack cache files produced during the build.

Looking at Next.js' config for Webpack, it looks like they're doing nothing particularly fancy with the Webpack cache, just enabling it: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/v13.4.2/packages/next/src/build/webpack-config.ts#L2515-L2531

Our Webpack config only makes one further change to the caching, reducing the lifetime to hopefully reduce the cache size by removing unused data: https://github.com/alveusgg/alveusgg/blob/edb7066578727af19d119df14b7719664f06ad58/apps/website/next.config.mjs#L277-L280

The only somewhat non-standard thing we're doing is processing videos in a Webpack loader w/ ffmpeg, but those are only around ~150MB in total, nowhere near close to the 1.00GB limit for the cache.

I have some notes here from my latest testing on this: https://github.com/alveusgg/alveusgg/pull/268#issuecomment-1545049469 -- the tl;dr here is that our entire actual output from the build process for the client/server sits in the ~260MB range, yet the Webpack cache sits at ~580MB when doing a fresh build, and grows if we do a build that uses the previous build's cache.

I have a few questions really that I'd love some help on:

- How can I trace what is in these cache files to understand why they're so large?
- What can I do to optimize the size of the cache, any tips?
- Why does the cache grow on sequential builds (`index.pack.old` files suddenly exist as well as `2.pack` and `3.pack`)? How can I ensure only the latest cache data is being kept on disk?

title: How to use the Worker filename generated by multi-compiler mode in the second stage?
body:
Similar to #14349 but I want to be able to use the output of the first stage of the multi-compiler mode in the second stage.

Let's say I have a simplified webpack config:
```js
[
  {
    name: "worker",
    target: "webworker",
    entry: "./worker.js",
    output: {
      path: path.join(__dirname, "dist"),
      filename: "worker-[contenthash].js"
    },
  },
  {
    name: "app",
    target: "browser",
    entry: "./app",
    output: {
      path: path.join(__dirname, "dist"),
      filename: "index.js"
    },
    dependencies: ["worker"],
  },
]
```
Then when we build, the first output, "worker" will be built and generate a file called `dist/worker-abc123.js`.

And inside of index.js I have:
```js
new Worker("<INSERT WORKER OUTPUT FILE HERE>")
```
How do I make it such that index.js will produce `new Worker("./worker-abc123.js")`?

To be honest, ignore the "dist" part if not relevant. I'm only really interested in passing the output filename of one into another. It's not even really about workers.

Is this possible?

## Workarounds
Currently I have some ideas for workarounds, namely having a plugin that searches the `dist` folder and finds the right file, and injects it into the `index.html` page. Or maybe using `ManifestPlugin` to generate a manifest that the second build looks for. But that seems like a lot.

title: Function support for __webpack_public_path__
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

I am requesting the addition of function support for the __webpack_public_path__ variable in webpack. Currently, __webpack_public_path__ is a variable that allows for setting a static public path at compile time. However, there are scenarios where the ability to dynamically compute the public path at runtime would be highly beneficial.

**What is the expected behavior?**

This would allow developers to provide a function that dynamically computes and returns the appropriate public path at runtime based on custom logic or environment variables.

For example:
```
__webpack_public_path__ = (fileName) => {
  // Custom logic to determine the public path based on the environment or configuration
  if(fileName.indexOf('javascript') != -1) {
    return "/assets/js"
  } else {
    return process.env.BASE_URL || '/';
  }
};
```

By introducing function support, webpack would call the function whenever it needs to determine the public path, allowing for runtime calculations and dynamic behavior.


**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**

The current limitation of __webpack_public_path__ being a static variable poses challenges in certain use cases. For example, when deploying an application to different environments or serving assets from different CDNs, it would be advantageous to calculate the public path dynamically based on the current environment or configuration. This would enable more flexibility and ease of deployment for different environments without the need for manual configuration changes.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

If function support is implemented for __webpack_public_path__, instead of directly assigning the public path, we could call a public path assignment function with the fileName or chunkId as an argument (__webpack_require__.p(chunkId)). This would allow us to dynamically compute and retrieve the appropriate public path based on the specific chunkId or fileName.
For example
```
var fullhref = __webpack_require__.p(chunkId) + href;
```
**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
No

title: Classic worker with ESM app
body:
I want to output ES modules for an app which uses a few workers.  However, I do not want the workers to use `import()` or `type: "module"`, i.e. they should remain "classic".  Reasons are:
1. Firefox support for ESM workers is still behind  a config flag, and until version 113 dynamic import was not supported
2. Experiencing hanging promises using `comlink` (which otherwise work fine with no ESM output)

The only way I see to attempt this is using `workerChunkLoading` as follows:

```js
const config = {
  target: "browserslist:modern", // supports dynamic import
  experiments: { outputModule: true },
  output: {
    module: true,
    workerChunkLoading: "import-scripts",
  }
}
```

However, this fails with a syntax error because the chunk format is still ESM for everything, even chunks only loaded by the worker.  Is there some way to force those chunks back to "array-push"?

It seems to me that a much more useful option would be `workerChunkFormat` and then set `workerChunkLoading` based on that, just as `chunkFormat` and `chunkLoading` behave.

title: Webpack doesn't minify export name on external module end when using `aurelia-bootstrapper` in production mode
body:
I'm encountering minification-related issue when bundling aurelia v1 app in production mode: it does not minify some dependencies on both ends, just import side, e.g code from my simple reproduction case:
```
import { ConsoleAppender } from 'aurelia-logging-console'
class CustomConsoleAppender extends ConsoleAppender { }
console.log(new CustomConsoleAppender())
```
bundles into something like
```
var i=n("aurelia-logging-console")
class r extends i.I{}
console.log(new r())
```
but the `i` doesn't contain `I` class, but originally named `ConsoleAppender` instead so I'm receiving undefined as the result.

The interesting thing is that when replacing import statement with
```
const { ConsoleAppender } = require('aurelia-logging-console')
```
it then doesn't minify export name on import side and thus works just fine.
Any ideas what might be wrong? Is it a webpack issue that should be reported?

body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

Can use some css post-processing loaders (like [css-modules-typescript-loader](https://github.com/seek-oss/css-modules-typescript-loader))，when I enable experiments.css.


**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
when I use css-loader, I can use `css-modules-typescript-loader` to create TypeScript declarations for CSS Modules.

This loader needs to be executed after css-loader because it needs to access the result of css-modules processing.

But, when I use experiments.css, I can't declare this loader after experiments.css processing.

```
module.exports = {
  module: {
    rules: [
      {
        test: /\.css$/,
        use: [
          'css-modules-typescript-loader',
          {
            loader: 'css-loader',
            options: {
              modules: true
            }
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
};
```

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**

In React component, I am using svg like this

When I am compiling this, 
Webpack is compiling it in this form

```
// CONCATENATED MODULE: ./src/Button/cart.svg
const cart_namespaceObject = __webpack_require__.p + "ee4d89699e3e3de82036.svg";
```

Now, the issue is, When I am trying to use this component in another consumer react Project which is also using webpack,

`__webpack_require__.p + "ee4d89699e3e3de82036.svg"; `
is not working properly,

The consumer's web pack is not changing any code or path here, and it's trying to access svg in the consumer's static assets location,
As that svg only exists inside the component library, its throwing an error.

Anything can be done for this one?

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

I am not sure if this is a bug or I am missing something.

1. Creating one component library in react using Webpack

3. When I am trying to access that SVG in some another React project which is also using webpack, I am getting 404 error for that SVG

title: Standalone API to build ModuleGraph of all entry points?
body:
I use https://github.com/aackerman/circular-dependency-plugin to get information around circular dependencies.

The plugins [works by tapping into the `optimizeModules` hook](https://github.com/aackerman/circular-dependency-plugin/blob/21777bf980e78cb55bff0c91e43a03bbfb37f762/index.js#L23), which looks to be [the first hook that `modules` is ready](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/56363993156e06a1230c9759eba277a22e6b6604/lib/Compilation.js#L2812). The plugin then recurses through the built `ModuleGraph` to find the circular dependencies.

However, running a full build seems to be a bit overkill just to get information around this `ModuleGraph`.

Is there a way to _only_ build this `ModuleGraph` without the overhead that comes with a full webpack compilation?

title: Webpack module federation issue
body:
I am running into an issue while integrating a microfrontend.

Error: "Shared module is not available for eager consumption: webpack/sharing/consume/default/redux/redux"

"webpack": "^5.85.1",
"webpack-bundle-analyzer": "^4.4.1",
"webpack-cli": "^5.1.3",
"webpack-dev-server": "^4.15.0"

I am attaching {eager: true} to every library it gives the error for.
For eg: I got the same error for "babel/ployfill". So I edited the webpack config as follows:

```
 new ModuleFederationPlugin({
name: "host",
filename: "remoteEntry.js",
remotes: {
   	remote:"remote@http://localhost:5500/remoteEntry.js"
},
 shared:{
 ...deps,
 redux:{eager:true},
"babel-polyfill": { eager: true }
 }
 })
 ```
This fixed the issue for "babel-polyfill" and redux library.

But then again, I got same error for "react-redux", "react", "prop-types" .. etc etc and I keep applying the above fix for all libraries.
This doesnt look like the best solution.

Has anyone found a better solution..? Please help!

title: How is [hash:base64] generated when using CSS modules and css-loader?
body:
I have tried to find out the answer to this on my own and haven't had the skills to get a reliable result.

We are using Laravel Mix to compile our assets and there appears to be a bug which doesn't allow defining `localIdentName`. This means that it _should_ be using the default `[hash:base64]` .

The question is, how is that hash actually generated? I thought it was doing a base64 hash of the class name with perhaps a salt but I couldn't find the salt and I couldn't recreate the generated class names by base64 encoding a variety of source strings. I tried :
- The class name
- class name + path
- class name + src
- Searching through code to find the string which is hashed
- Looking for where the base64 encode happens

My hope is that if I know how the hashes are created I can recreate the process in PHP and enable scoped styles that way. Also this has now become a scratch to itch and learn how this is done. Any input/help is greatly appreciated.

https://github.com/laravel-mix/laravel-mix/issues/3359

title: Dynamically load highest minor version in shared scope
body: In a page, we are planning to load three apps in the shared scope. App 1 and App 2 are currently using React version 17.0.1 as their build version. Now, we intend to add App 3, which will use React version 17.0.2. However, there is a possibility that App 3 might inadvertently use the 17.0.1 version already present in the shared scope. Is there a way to dynamically load the highest minor version available in the shared scope during runtime?
author: ssmilin

title: SSR and lazyCompilation
body:
I've already asked this question [before](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions/15698) but didn't get the answer and hope this is worth another try.

Is there any way to make `lazyCompilation` feature work with SSR. SSR requires from us to have info about used chunks on page to build the html page, but obviously lazy compiled chunks are not in the client's stats file.

More details on the issue is in the [repo](https://github.com/meskill/webpack-lazy-compilation), please take a look in it.

title: Webpack 4 to 5 Migration Question - Updating Outdated Options
body:
Hello friends!

So Im working on Wp4 to Wp5 migration, and Im currently using the official Migration Guide - and specifically, Im on this step: https://webpack.js.org/migrate/5/#update-outdated-options

I have some reservations about updating this specific outdated option: `optimization.splitChunks.cacheGroups.vendors → optimization.splitChunks.cacheGroups.defaultVendors`

**For more context, Im on the latest version of Wp 4**: `4.46.0` - and there are no references to `defaultVendors` in the codebase - which is not what I would have expected

The default `vendors` cacheGroup is still being referenced in the codebase instead - `lib/WebpackOptionsDefaulter.js`
- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/v4.46.0/lib/WebpackOptionsDefaulter.js#L251
```
this.set("optimization.splitChunks.cacheGroups.vendors", {
	automaticNamePrefix: "vendors",
	test: /[\\/]node_modules[\\/]/,
	priority: -10
});
```

Am I correct to assume that **updating these outdated options should happen before upgrading to Webpack 5?**

Or should they be updated during the Webpack 5 upgrade?

title: Generating multiple parameterized outputs of a given entrypoint
body:
I'm working on a large application where we run Webpack `n` times in parallel to produce `n` builds, one that is specific to each locale that we support. A simplified explanation of the way we do this is to have the JS import from a `translations` directory full of JSON files, and then in each parameterized build we add a `resolve.alias` rule to point the import to the specific locale that's being built for.

I'm wondering if there's a better way. The JSON files don't have any imports themselves, so they'll always be leaf nodes, so I imagine the compilation graph will look the same for each of these builds. Is there a way to run Webpack in such a way that when it encounters these particular imports, it could generate `n` different chunks, one for each locale, instead of running `n` different full webpack builds?

Here is some sample code showing an example of the current approach: https://github.com/ryanwilsonperkin/locale-specific-builds
author: ryanwilsonperkin

title: Bundle third package source code and tree shaking.
body:
I create a package called `whatplatformis` https://github.com/JiangWeixian/whatplatformis , it can be used in this way

```ts
import { isServer } "whatplatformis"
```

`isServer` will be `true` on node side and `false` on browser side with `condition exports`. When I used it in `file-a.tsx`

```tsx
// file-a.tsx
import { isServer } "whatplatformis"
if (isServer) {
  console.log('isServer')
}
```

`console.log('isServer')` will be `treeshaking` when build target is `web`. **But when I used in `file-b.tsx` again.**

```tsx
// file-b.tsx
import { isServer } "whatplatformis"
if (isServer) {
  console.log('isServer')
}
```

the bundle result will be: 

```js
c&&console.error("isServer")
```
`c` is module `whatplatformis`, `whatplatformis` will be bundle into common chunks. `treeshaking` is failed. So there is anyway to make `splitChunks` and `treeshaking` work?

title: In react js app, subpath url ("/register") not working giving error "page not found"  after deploy build on vercel or netlify but working fine in local devserver.
body:
I am creating on a MERN stack project where i created react js using webpack5, you can see my webpack.config in [here](https://github.com/growupanand/crm-webapp). In this app i am using react-router-dom for routes. Everything works fine when i run this app in local devserver using `webpack-dev-server`. Then i deploy it in vercel, it is working, app is loading fine when i click on register button to redirect to "/redirect" page it worked. But after loaded register page if i refresh page it says 404 error page not found or if i go directly to "/register" page it gives same 404 error.

I tried adding vercel.json file (which is also in my repo) to rewrite URL request to index.html. Subpath are now working fine.

But now "proxy" for API request not working. For this there is one solution I found is telling API request rewrite in vercel.json which is also tested by me and working fine, but the problem with this is i cant use env variables in vercel.json to tell backend endpoint, so for this i have to hardcode my backend endpoint in repo which is not a good practice.

include it: include the module, evaluate it and continue analysing dependencies
skip over: don't include it, don't evaluate it but continue analysing dependencies
exclude it: don't include it, don't evaluate it and don't analyse dependencies

author: k4mr4n

title: webpackJsonpCallback is not called when I navigate to a new page
body:
I am facing a weird issue when I try to load new chunks while navigating to a new page, which I am not sure what's the root cause of it. I have implemented code splitting in my app and the build runs okay, the chunks are created and the app loads fine on the initial load. However, when I navigate to a new page, the app fails with a `ChunkLoad` error. Initially, I thought that the location of the chunks was invalid, which I checked, but it seems alright. The HTTP calls on the network tab also show a 200 status code. I need to mention that the app runs on dev mode, which cancels any scenario of a caching issue.

I spent a bit of time reading the build code and I noticed that `webpackJsonpCallback` is never called when I move to a new page, which is not the case when the app loads for a first time. If I understood correctly, `webpackJsonpCallback` is responsible for registering a chunk in the modules and `installedChunks` stores. If the chunk doesn't exists in `installedChunks`, it is marked as failure by the `loadingEnded` callback, which runs as soon as the chunk loading is done. `__webpack_require__.f.j` is the method responsible for creating the JSONP script, injecting it to the DOM and attaching the `loadingEnded` callback.

Can anyone think of reason why `webpackJsonpCallback` is never called when I navigate to a new page?

module: {

      test: /\.m?(t|j)sx?/,

I've tried adding `use: ["swc-loader"]` to the first rule but the output is the same.

Does `asset/resource` not do import/type processing?

body:
Hi there,

I'm trying to put together a development setup using Docker, Nginx and Webpack. Everything works fine, but now I'm trying to add Nginx's JavaScript module to filter the response body that comes from Webpack Dev Server.

I tested this on a React project using Vite and there it works fine, but now I'm porting an old project using Create React App, which of course uses Webpack under the hood, but I'm getting this error: "js exception: InternalError: invalid UTF-8 string".

On the browser:

![webpack](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/48509601/dfd16637-c616-4cae-843f-74a6f5318ad2)

Specifically this error happens when I try to run this line of code, a simple string replace to inject some additional tags to the HTML head.

```js
const updatedResponse = chunk.replace(/(<\/head>)/, newData + '$1');
```

I managed to get some logs of the response from WDS and it looks like this:

```json
{
  "timestamp": "2023-07-31T13:42:03+00:00",
  "connection": {
    "serial": 1,
    "request_count": 1,
    "elapsed_time": 0.009,
    "pipelined": false
  },
  "request": {
    "client": "172.18.0.1",
    "port": 80,
    "host": "localhost",
    "method": "GET",
    "uri": "/",
    "http_version": 1.1,
    "bytes_received": 473,
    "headers": {
      "User-Agent": "Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64; rv:109.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/115.0",
      "Connection": "keep-alive",
      "Cache-Control": "no-cache",
      "Host": "localhost:3000",
      "Accept": "text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9,image/avif,image/webp,*/*;q=0.8",
      "Upgrade-Insecure-Requests": "1",
      "Sec-Fetch-Site": "cross-site",
      "Accept-Language": "en-US,en;q=0.5",
      "Sec-Fetch-Dest": "document",
      "Accept-Encoding": "gzip, deflate, br",
      "DNT": "1",
      "Pragma": "no-cache",
      "Sec-Fetch-Mode": "navigate"
    }
  },
  "response": {
    "status": 200,
    "bytes_sent": 1892,
    "headers": {
      "Content-Type": "text/html; charset=UTF-8",
      "X-Powered-By": "Express",
      "Accept-Ranges": "bytes",
      "Vary": "Accept-Encoding",
      "Content-Encoding": "gzip"
    }
  }
}
```

Unfortunately I'm not very sure if this can provide some useful information to debug this problem, although I suspect the response header of "Accept-Ranges: bytes" may have something to do with it, since it seems to be most commonly used for downloading files?

Anyway, my question is how does the Webpack Dev Server handle serving files? Is there some encoding or transformation that I need to do if I need to intercept the request in between?

Thank you!
author: D10f

title: Why does Webpack tree-shake this module that's marked with side-effects?
body:
Hello,

I am wondering why a piece of code gets flagged as dead and eliminated from production compilation builds. I have read webpack's documentation about optimization, especially sideEffects, but can't see exactly what's causing it to now be seen as unused. The code wasn't eliminated in v4, but now is in v5.

Check out the `Auth` import in this repo example:
https://github.com/RyanofWoods/webpack-aws-debug/blob/c01f49e1b1a95d05143b547552d711252d44ac05/index.js#L1

In the development compilation, you can see that it imports `node_modules/@aws-amplify/auth/lib-esm/Auth.js` and more specifically `Amplify.register` due to the `Auth` import in `index.js`. However, it does not occur in the production compilation due to tree-shaking.

Why does `Auth` get seen as unused, although its marked as having side-effects?

`@aws-amplify` re-exports `Auth` from `@aws-amplify/auth`.
https://github.com/aws-amplify/amplify-js/blob/1b8c8e2814b283384ad48c8aff5aecc9f3953d67/packages/aws-amplify/src/index.ts#L33

`@aws-amplify` set its `sideEffects` as `false`.
https://github.com/aws-amplify/amplify-js/blob/1b8c8e2814b283384ad48c8aff5aecc9f3953d67/packages/aws-amplify/package.json#L11

And `@aws-amplify/auth` sets the `Auth` file as having sideEffects:
https://github.com/aws-amplify/amplify-js/blob/1b8c8e2814b283384ad48c8aff5aecc9f3953d67/packages/auth/package.json#L11-L17

---

Note I have tried setting `./customerPool.js` and `./index.js` as having `sideEffects` within `package.json`, as well as importing Auth more directly into `./index.js`, and even being more specific with the import (`@aws-amplify/auth`).
author: RyanofWoods

title: webpack5 module federation: idToExternalAndNameMapping missing and not work with ts-loader and CommonJS module ?
body:
# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

The project source are ts files. It's webpack config uses ts-loader to build. Here's the error (can't resolve remote modules) on runtime.

(node:1249) UnhandledPromiseRejectionWarning: TypeError: Cannot read property 'call' of undefined
    at __webpack_require__ (/Users/sam/dev/my_mf_nodejs/app1/dist/main.js:192:42)
    at Object.560 (/Users/sam/dev/my_mf_nodejs/app1/dist/main.js:46:17)
    at __webpack_require__ (/Users/sam/dev/my_mf_nodejs/app1/dist/main.js:192:42)
    at /Users/sam/dev/my_mf_nodejs/app1/dist/main.js:68:45

main.js: 192:42: __webpack_modules__[moduleId].call(module.exports, module, module.exports, __webpack_require__);
 
I noticed "var idToExternalAndNameMapping" is missing in bundle js genernated by webpack. However, it works fine if "module": "ESNext" is added to tsconfig.json. That means default or CommonJS module format does not work.

**Steps to reproduce:**
1. clone [the repo](https://github.com/yumingsam/module-federation-nodejs-example)
2. checkout branch "ts_cjs_broken"
3. cd app1
4. npm i
5. npm start
6.  see "idToExternalAndNameMapping" is not generated in app1/dist/main.js
7. cd app2
8. npm i
9. npm start
10. it shows the error message
11. enable "module": "ESNext" in app1/tsconfig.json, then repeat steps 3~9, there's no error and console outputs the result from remote modues of app2.


**Other relevant information:**
webpack version: 5.74.0, 5.88.2
Node.js version: 14.18.1
Operating System: macos 13.4.1

title: Do we still need records now?
body:
After reviewing some information, it's mentioned that records are used to ensure the stability of building chunks( filename). However, when using the chunkHash to name built results in production mode, the filenames of the chunks are already guaranteed to be stable, Even if AggressiveSplitPlugin is used.
In this case, what other purposes can records serve? 

PS: I've looked into the webpack source code, and it doesn't seem to utilize records for any specific build optimization. It simply checks if a records file exists, reads its content, and provides it to plugins through hooks.

title: Obtaining Path Metadata for Asset Modules
body:
Hello Webpack people,

Lets say I have `foo.jpg` and it lives in `my-project/images/foo.jpg`

And my Webpack output path is `dist/`

Now lets process this with a Webpack rule like this:
```
rules: [
    {
      test: /\.(jpg)$/i,
      type: 'asset/resource',
      generator: {
        filename: '[name].[hash][ext]',
      },
    },
]
```

In my `dist/` folder, I'll now have the processed image. So something like this `foo.jpg => foo.123456.jpg`

**So my question is this:** Is it possible that I can obtain the absolute path of `foo.jpg` and its Webpack equivalent either during Webpack build, or post-build with a plugin? If yes, which source code files should I be looking at?

Some context: Im looking for ways to server side render these asset modules - Im using babel on the server side. So, Im not bundling the server with Webpack (which I think would solve my issue, but its a longer solution to implement for a business) and the issue is locating the Webpack bundled assets like images.
author: nsunga

title: Dynamic import doesn't work for content scripts in a Chrome extension
body:
I'm developing a Chrome extension that has several features. One of them consists of a content script that adds nodes to the DOM of a given page. However, as the content script runs in a sandboxed context relative to the main page context, the dynamic imports that are needed to load an icon are not working.

### Repository
https://github.com/cleydyr/test-webpack-chrome-extension

### Instructions

1. Clone the repository
2. Run `npm i` on the project root
3. Run `npm start` (or `webpack  --config webpack.config.js` if you have it installed globally)
4. Import the extension contained in the `dist` folder into Chrome using [the Load unpacked function](https://developer.chrome.com/docs/extensions/mv3/getstarted/development-basics/#load-unpacked)
5. Load any page at http://localhost (it can be anything returning an HTML page).

### Expected result
A single download icon will replace the page at http://localhost.

### Current result
An error can be seen in the browser console:

```
jsonp chunk loading:27 Uncaught (in promise) ChunkLoadError: Loading chunk icon.download failed.
(missing: chrome-extension://opcdnnmdmghcbcjbjnphimfllgmblkeg/icon.download.js)
    at __webpack_require__.f.j (jsonp chunk loading:27:1)
    at ensure chunk:6:1
    at Array.reduce (<anonymous>)
    at __webpack_require__.e (ensure chunk:5:1)
    at Array.map (<anonymous>)
    at webpackAsyncContext (.*$ chunkName: icon.[request] namespace object:3819:1)
    at EuiIconClass.loadIconComponent (icon.js:102:1)
    at EuiIconClass.componentDidMount (icon.js:146:1)
    at commitLayoutEffectOnFiber (react-dom.development.js:23310:1)
    at commitLayoutMountEffects_complete (react-dom.development.js:24688:1)
```

This is because the `webpackJsonpCallback` function is bound to the `self` object from the content script context, whereas the actual script, which is loaded by attaching a script tag to the page DOM, gets the self from the main page context, which is a different one from the content script's.

Is there a workaround for this behavior?

title: Modify entrypoints by AST result?
body:
Hi brains trust, I'm trying to find a way to create entrypoints dynamically, based on some metasyntax within my code. 

For example, my directory structure might look like this:
```
src/
 |- page1/
      |- script1.ts
      |- script2.ts
 |- page2/
      |- script3.ts
      |- script4.ts
index.ts
```

The metasyntax (in this case, Typescript decorators), look something like this:

```typescript
// src/page1/script1.ts
@entrypoint({ url: '/some/url' })
export class SomeClass { ... }

// src/page2/script3.ts
@entrypoint({ url: '/somewhere/else' })
export class SomeOtherClass { ... }
```

I have `index.ts` sourcing the files I want as part of my bundle, for typescript to correctly infer what is and isn't being used. However, what I would like to be able to do is dynamically parse the syntax tree and generate an entrypoint for `page1/script1.ts` and `page2/script3.ts`, based on the fact that it has identified the `@entrypoint( ... )` code.  Manually writing the entrypoints in the webpack config is not an option (there would be tens, if not hundreds, at this point).

The goal is to allow for reduce code size by chunking scripts into only what needs to be loaded per page. The bundle currently comes in at 2-3mb (including vendor libs), regardless of what scripts may need to be run, when parsing the URLs at runtime, and I would like to try and just perform the minimum.

I've tried to figure out the best way to handle this with dynamic `import`s, or using something like https://github.com/sysgears/webpack-virtual-modules, but I'm at a loss for how to best generate these entrypoints. 

Additionally, I would like to keep being able to use `webpack --watch` on the files, parsing the syntax changes as necessary.

Keen to hear what people suggest!

title: How to improve home page performance (merge chunks) after using module federation on library sharing? (Maybe HTTP2 is not so fine)
body: At first, this is not a bug. The webpack version I use: 5.88.1. I work in a company who's business logic of products are very complex. Micro-Frontend architecture is quite common in my company, making module federation (MF) a suitable choice for us. Thanks MF. We've been using MF for over a year now, and it has worked seamlessly for sharing React components across multiple projects, albeit without any shared libraries. This year, our focus is on enhancing the performance of various products spanning multiple projects through Micro-Frontend. We have started sharing UI components in Micro-Frontend across these projects, with each component being packaged as an isolated npm package. To enable the sharing of any component, webpack(MF plugin) have split the code of each component into separate chunks. Initially, we didn't see any issues with having numerous chunks, as we believed in the power of HTTP2 (for HTTP2 multiplexing). We conducted multiple tests in both local and online user environments, collecting and analyzing performance data. Our findings indicated that a smaller number of chunks significantly improves performance. To address this, we made efforts to merge chunks using the optimization.splitChunks.cacheGroups option. However, it seems that when sharing components with Module Federation, the code splitting for these components takes precedence. We tried various options, but the results were not as expected. One day, we found that `eager` option (of MF shared option) may help us. After setting `eager: true` for all the components the home page of a product(project), the number of chunks reduced noticeably (e.g. index.xxxx.js gets larger), resulting in improved performance. However, we noticed that the MF remoteEntry.js file also increased in size. After a deep dive into the eager option documentation, we realized this was expected but not desirable for us. We consider remoteEntry.js as a resource list, similar to an HTML role, and expect it to have a small code size. Consumer projects load remoteEntry.js from another provider project using a timestamp (e.g., remoteEntry.js?t=160xxxxx) or with a no-cache HTTP header to access the latest code. While other resources (like index.[contenthash].js) use long-term caching from CDN. If remoteEntry.js becomes too large, it could negatively impact performance. Just like this demo: https://github.com/module-federation/module-federation-examples/tree/master/shared-routes2/app2, after building in the dist directory, `remoteEntry.js` and `main.js` both have `react` and `react-dom` source code. It appears that `eager: true` may not be the ideal solution for us to enhance performance, and using a no-cache strategy for remoteEntry.js may not be the best practice. Then, We want to build two versions of source business code, one version with `eager: true` and without `exposes`, another one version without `eager: true` and with `exposes`, maybe it will work, but it is too heavy. Could someone provide some advice on this matter? Thank you! This image helps illustrate the usage of MF in our company:
author: stoneChen

title: splitChunks behavior is affected by cacheGroups.name field
body:
## Example

Say we have a module relationship like this:

```
index --dyn import()--> a --import--> x, y
index --dyn import()--> b --import--> x
```

Before splitChunks, we have 2 chunks here, one for `a` and one for `b`.

We set cacheGroups.default.text to `/x|y/`, so `x` and `y` both match, and will be split later (assume that we match the minSize here).

The default strategy of splitChunks, will split `x` in a common chunk , which is expected as it shows up in 2 chunks. And `y` still only appear in chunk `a`.

But if we set cacheGroups.default.name to any value, `x` and `y` appear in the same chunk. If `x` and `y` are css modules, this behavior will cause some priority problems.

![image](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/57202839/7dd9834b-ae86-4a71-b55c-7e5a7d77805d)


## Code Sandbox

https://codesandbox.io/p/sandbox/bold-orla-5xqm5d?file=%2Foutput%2Findex.js%3A12%2C19

run `npm run build`, see the result, and comment or uncomment cacheGroups.default.name, see the differences

## Questions

I know the reason in source code, its because the key of `chunkInfoMap` is affected by name if specified, otherwise it is based on the chunks combinations.

But I do not know if this is by design or a bug, personally I feel confused if name affects splitChunks results

title: Intermittent "Module parse failed" errors
body:
I work on a large scale project where webpack runs in CI hundreds of times per day. For the last few weeks, we are seeing an intermittent but persistent rate (~1%) of "Module parse failed" errors with unterminated strings, regular expressions, and unexpected input. In all cases, the parsed input doesn't match what's actually on disk. In some cases parsed input seems truncated while in others parsed input has appended content. Actual file contents on disk seem valid in all cases, and don't change between successful and unsuccessful runs.

This seems completely unrelated to obvious loader config issues that would lead to consistently reproducible failures.  It happens with completely different webpack loader configs and there seem to be no common loaders involved. I don't think it could be a broad issue with file I/O or handles as we run a wide variety of processes and build tasks and none fail with similar file I/O issues. As a result, this seems to be some sort of Node or webpack architectural issue. It seems like there is some type of pollution or corruption of global state or file streams that's causing these intermittent types of errors. I wanted to post here in case anyone has ever encountered anything remotely similar as it's been a real head scratcher for us.

We do have many bundles running in parallel on these VMs in CI, but they are running in their own webpack CLI-invoked Node processes and should be isolated from each other. I'm curious if anybody knows of any way that state could somehow pollute across bundles happening in separate processes.

I could also use any help trying to pinpoint the issue more closely, such as tracing loader chain activity, printing file contents/stats, tracing parsed input, etc.

webpack: 5.82.0, 5.88.2
Node: v18.17.1
OS: Ubuntu 22.04.2 LTS

```
Module parse failed: Unterminated regular expression (2:1)
File was processed with these loaders:
* ../../node_modules/source-map-loader/dist/cjs.js
* ../../node_modules/@griffel/webpack-extraction-plugin/src/webpackLoader.js
You may need an additional loader to handle the result of these loaders.
| export {};
> /
```

```
Module parse failed: Unterminated string constant (30:14)
You may need an appropriate loader to handle this file type, currently no loaders are configured to process this file. See https://webpack.js.org/concepts#loaders
| export * from "./createServiceContainer";
|
> export * from "./MarginProperty

title: Doesn't work for workspace / common dependencies.
body:
# Bug report

As far as I can tell webpack currently doesn't work very well in monorepos where projects depend on each other.

**What is the current behavior?**

Assume you have 2 packages in your monorepo

 - `monorepo/packages/common`
 - `monorepo/packages/project`        <- Webpack project that has `common` as a dependency

The monorepo/package.json has
```
"workspaces": [
  "packages/common",
  "packages/project",
],
```

and `monorepo/packages/project/package.json` has

```
"dependencies": {
  "@monorepo/common": "1.0.0",
}
```

As part of the build process webpack will create a package.json in `.webpack/dependencies` with the minimal dependencies it needs and runs `yarn install --non-interactive --frozen-lockfile`. Now because `monorepo/packages/common/.webpack/dependencies` isn't in the list of workspaces in `monorepo/package.json` yarn will not look for  `@monorepo/common` in the workspace, and instead tries to find it on yarnpkg.org, where it doesn't exist, resulting in `
error An unexpected error occurred: "https://registry.yarnpkg.com/@monorepo/common: Not found".`.

You can get around it by adding the webpack directory, but that seems very hacky / brittle

```
"workspaces": [
  "packages/common",
  "packages/project",
  "packages/common/.webpack/dependencies"        <- new
],
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

See steps above

**What is the expected behavior?**

Webpack bundling should work in a monorepo with shared dependencies

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  webpack@5.88.2
Node.js version: v18.16.0
Yarn version: v1.22.19
Operating System: Mac OS Ventura 13.4.1
Additional tools: 

-----

NB: I'm doing this through the Serverless Framework, so there's a chance it's specific to that, although from what I can tell it's a webpack issue.

body:
> Hi, i ask same question in redux-saga repo but i think there is some build step details
> https://github.com/redux-saga/redux-saga/issues/2396

I'm having difficulty building a project for Node.js via webpack.

In my case in the project I use my own package that uses redux-saga as dependency

In webpack I specified that my package should be included in the bundle via webpack-node-externals allowList option.

After build and run app in Node.js it crashes with error exactly in the place where my package uses redux-saga, at this place error is: createSagaMiddleware is not a function

In the same time if i import redux-saga directly in project just like it's done in package - it will works perfectly

I see that CJS and ESM versions of redux-saga is different because if i just copy code that uses redux-saga from my package to project - it will works fine

### Repro

I prepare repo with exmaple of issue:
https://github.com/krutoo/webpack-externals-breaks-isomorph

just install dependencies and run npx webpack watch --mode=development

### Details

As i understand:

- external code uses `require('redux-saga')` that use `exports` object as function instead its "default" property
- bundled code uses ESM version that uses default import as function

The output of webpack config 2 is loaded through a host app which we don't have control over. The override bundle is used for loading a override bundle though config 2 instead of the production bundle for dev testing. I am seeing this issue when loading the override bundle.

title: Making a sprite loader for Webpack
body:
I have created a sprite loader for Webpack, which works well in most use cases. However, there are still some issues that need to be addressed.

The loader exports the following functionality:

1. A pitch function: This function assumes that all the images are collected and organized by directory name.

2. A standard loader function: Within this function, it waits until all the images within a group are ready, then packs them into sprite sheets and emits the result. Finally, it returns a JavaScript that defaults to exporting an object containing the position and dimensions of the image, along with the URLs of the sprite sheets.


There are a few issues I'd like to address:

**Support for CSS**: I attempted to support CSS by using a PostCSS plugin, but it seems that the postcss-loader runs before my custom loader. My loader is configured as follows:

```
{
  test: [/\.jpe?g$/, /\.png$/],
  resourceQuery: /sprites/,
  use: [
    {
      loader: require.resolve('../image/loader.js')
    }
  ]
}
```

**Webpack Loader Limitations**: There's a limitation with Webpack loaders. For instance, if there are 120 images under the same directory imported through `require.context('@/dir/?sprites')`, I observed that the pitch loader processes the first 100 images, followed by the normal loader. The remaining 20 images are not processed in the pitch loader, resulting in sprite sheets generated for images 1-100, while the remaining 20 are processed in a second round.

**Caching Image Processing**: Anyone have ideas on how to implement image processing caching? Perhaps hashing all the input images and performing comparisons? This could potentially help improve performance."

Thanks for your reading

title: dynamic imports with name-hinted css chunks
body:
I have an app that manages multiple iframes. I know which components & css files go to which frame based on their location on disk. (i.e. frame1Componnets/ & frame2Components/). I successfully use SplitChunks / cacheGroups to generate frame-specific CSS files by testing for css files within the folders.

Instead, I want to use code-splitting to have smaller chunks and still route CSS files to the right frame.

I've found that MiniCssExtractPlugin.insert can take a function, so I can manage insertion of the link tag to the right frame.  Now, I just need to be able to figure out the target frame given a link tag -- or in other words, given the filename of the css file.

That's where I'm stuck.  MiniCssExtractPlugin.chunkFilename has chunk metadata, but it doesn't contain a list of files, or a the raw contents of the chunk, making it hard to figure out what frame its for. It would be nice if I could somehow use SplitChunks / cacheGroups to add to the idNameHints array, then use those hints in MiniCssExtractPlugin.chunkFilename, but not quite sure how to do that.

Is what I'm trying to do even possible?  Am I using the wrong plugins for this?  Am I missing something in the metadata?
