title: Webpack node module getting added in multiple chunks in my react application
body:
In my react application, I am using `react-addons-css-transition-group` node module. In the prod build, webpack (5.36.2) is clubbing this module in multiple chunks and not with the vendor even though I have defined vendor splitChunks cacheGroup config as node_modules folder.

`vendor: /[\\/]node_modules[\\/]/,`

```
splitChunks: {
  name: false,
  cacheGroups: {
    commons:  {
      test: {
        vendor: /[\\/]node_modules[\\/]/,
      },
      name: 'vendor',
      chunks: 'initial'
    },
    default: {
      minChunks: 2,
      reuseExistingChunk: true,
      minSize: 100000
    }
  }
}
```

My question is why isn't webpack making a single chunk for this module so it can be loaded in all pages making use of it?

Also sometimes it clubs this module with components that are not needed in a particular page but get loaded through the chunk because of the node module. How should we deal with this?

PS: my vendor is 86kB gzip currently and some npm modules are clubbed in other chunks with my source code. My problem is not specific to react-addons-css-transition-group. Its happening for lot of other modules too.

<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

It is not adding a node module in one chunk only and replicating in multiple? Sometimes it clubs it with code that is only required in page B lets say but gets loaded in page A because of reference to the node module.

<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->
<!-- Repositories with too many files or large `webpack.config.js` files are not suitable -->
<!-- Please only add small code snippets directly into this issue -->
<!-- https://gist.github.com is a good place for longer code snippets -->
<!-- If your issue is caused by a plugin or loader, please create an issue on the loader/plugin repository instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**

Ideally webpack should put all node modules in vendor bundle as specified in config. But since its not putting every node module there I suspect it might be due to already heavy size of vendor 86kb GZIP and hence remaining node modules are being clubbed in other chunks. But chunking is not logical as it should prevent clubbing modules with source code that is specific to a particular page and could accidentally ship to other pages too due to this.

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  5.36.2
Node.js version: 12.18.3

author: VishalGulati

title: Suggestion for improve the code splitting mechanism
body:
Hi, everyone)
The diagram illustrates the architecture and request flow:
![image](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/96373865/68186636-ce63-460e-9c78-d930c0fcbcbb)
We need a simple way to separate the JS bundle of the solution into different folders. This way, in the BFF, we can check the requested part of the FE by root: if the request is for the content of a folder, for example, '/secure/..', then we can verify at the BFF level whether the user is authenticated.
This is necessary so that we do not return the entire SPA with potentially sensitive information for unauthenticated users.
Do you plan to enhance the code splitting mechanism to specify the folder where js chunks should be placed?) Do you have any recommendations?)

title: Issue with asset/modules, while loading the image through css, with frontend and backend server being different.
body:
Before I explain the issue that I have in my case, here is my general info:
![webpack-cli INFO](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/107140611/3dc47c48-4d31-417c-b6f0-f12f76d30afc)

**Now, here is my project description:**

- I am developing a Vue application.
- I am using tailwind-css in my application.
- my back-end is on python flask.
- I am using xampp apache to serve my application on : ( URL:  http://127.0.0.1:80/)
- And my Front-end web-pack server runs on: (URL: http://127.0.0.1:8080/bundle.js)
- Therefore my Project structure is something like this:

![project-struc](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/107140611/5aa63dae-4ab6-4421-812a-a7cc3ae99033)


**Now coming back to problem.**
So far everything was working fine, since I'm using web-pack V-5, I was aware that the url-loader and file-loader are depreciated, and instead asset/modules are introduced. Therefore, that's what I used, here is my webpack.config.js :

![webpackconfig](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/107140611/660f8187-c254-436c-a41e-a47578ae6734)

Even after that when I run my project I, couldn't load my Image to the project:

![Browser debugger image prob](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/107140611/a744987a-0042-4af9-a60c-6cf6d721de6b)

Now, here is what I found, the problem was never with web-pack or with my path inside my main.css file which has property(background-image) with url for the image. I found that the problem is with the dynamic URL with hash for image that is loaded by the web-pack, since I am using a different PORT ( 8080 ) for my bundle.js, the web-pack loads the file with URL without the port number, which creates a problem. Here, is how when I change or add port number to URL, image seems to be loading.

![Screenshot 2024-05-21 183755](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/assets/107140611/9b3e44d4-a4d1-4b54-9ed9-6a43e71e65a7)

**Now here is my question:**

Is there a way I can give correct port to the web-pack so that it knows the correct URL to reach my asset ? or if not then what should be my approach, given that my back-end would remain on python, since I have specific use for the language. 

Thanks~

title: Allow strictExportPresence to work with babel-loader or ts-loader
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template or we'll close your issue -->

## Feature request

<!-- Issues that contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->
<!-- Check if this feature needs to be implemented in a plugin or loader instead -->
<!-- If yes: file the issue on the plugin/loader repo -->
<!-- Features related to the development server should be filed on this repo instead -->

**What is the expected behavior?**
strictExportPresence should check to see if imports are valid in javascript files, and using a loader shouldn't disable this flag.

**What is motivation or use case for adding/changing the behavior?**
It's common for people to use loaders and strictExportPresence is a very helpful flag.

**How should this be implemented in your opinion?**
My understanding is that babel-loader or ts-loader changes files in-place and then passes them on to the webpack parser, which is why `strictExportPresence` is configured under parser/javascript. My understanding is probably wrong but I don't really know why the setting does nothing if you use a loader.

**Are you willing to work on this yourself?**
yes if someone can explain to me why this is happening

title: Speeding up hot-reloads of webpack-dev-server
body: **Versions:** Webpack version: 5.91.0 (dev-server and dev-middleware are also at latest released versions). Node version: 20.5.1 **Problem** Our webpack configuration is for 259 entrypoints. We use webpack-dev-server in development and making any change takes 6-8 seconds to recompile, even when only adding or removing a blank line to a single file. I took a node profile and things that stick out are `buildChunkGraph` taking 1.7 seconds and `SplitChunksPlugin` taking 2.5 seconds. Profile: [webpack-51-hot-empty-lines.cpuprofile](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/15518871/webpack-51-hot-empty-lines.cpuprofile) If I change our config to only use 20 entrypoints then things are much faster. It seems like the reload is redoing a bunch of work considering no imports are being changed (so there shouldn't be any changes to chunking) - is there any way I can configure webpack to say "hey don't try to change / recalculate chunks as part of a hot reload"? I'd also welcome any advice on what to look into further or ways to get more actionable logs / profiles.

title: ModuleFederation conflict with entry output
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
ModuleFederation chunk conflict with Entry chunk
**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

```js
// index.ts
export const a = 1;
export const b = 2;
export const c = 3;

// remote.js
import { a } from '.'

// main.js
import { b, c } from '.'
```
I set `main.js` to entry and expose `remote.js` to ModuleFederation.

They will use same moduleId to generate code in chunks.

But `remote.js` chunks will haven't  exports `b` and `c`  because of  tree-shaking.

So, if loaded remoteEntry.js first, webpack runtime will use moduleId to cacheModule, and then loaded `main.js` will can't find constant `b` or `c`.


<!-- A great way to do this is to provide your configuration via a GitHub repository -->
<!-- The most helpful is a minimal reproduction with instructions on how to reproduce -->

title: Webpack roadmap regarding module federation 2.0
body:
Hello! Module Federation version 2.0 (https://module-federation.io/blog/announcement.html) has recently been released. Now that it is decoupled from Webpack, I wonder: Does the Webpack team plan to completely remove the internal plugin from Webpack's module federation, or will it still be available? And finally, if it is kept as a Webpack plugin, will it have the same updates as version 2.0 or will the implementation be the same as it is now?

Thanks!

title: v5 is not faster than v4 when performing secondary packaging
body: The project was migrated from v4 to v5. After testing, it was found that multiple builds without code modification did not speed up the project. In addition, the number of files in node_modules\.cache\webpack increased even though the code did not change. I guess it might be due to an error in hash calculation? How can I troubleshoot the problem?

title: How to use mini-css-extract-plugin with webpack built libraries in a monorepo?
body: I have a non-cli Angular yarn workspaces / lerna monorepo. There is a mixture of non-angular typescript libraries built with rollup and some "app module" **libraries with Angular components that are built with webpack**. Then of course the final app package built with webpack. I can get mini-css-extract-plugin to work with the final app package, but if I use it for the "app module" libraries then styles don't load nor are there 404s or anything indicating missing files. Is this even supported? Does the final app package put dependency styles in it's extracted css (I dont' think so)

author: cengizilhan

title: how does implicit resolution of nested node_modules folders work?
body:
I have found an issue where in certain conditions, webpack with sass-loader using the sass implementation works but sass-embedded does not, and initially raised the issue with example in sass-loader (https://github.com/webpack-contrib/sass-loader/issues/1203). Based on the below, I've some questions about how webpack resolution works for nested node_modules folders

Certain conditions are that you have a dependency in a nested `node_modules` folder of a dependency, and there is also a dependency called by more than one of the other dependencies (unrelated to the nested one) - the demo repo below has a basic mockup of this structure

As the findings in sass-loader where that what is returned from `prev` in sass-embedded were wrong, I put together a second example that used sass and sass-embedded without the use of loaders to help the owners of sass debug.

However, with this I found that neither sass nor sass-embedded are finding the nested `node_modules` folder without it being explicitly set in `includePaths` (which wasn't a big surprise). As the place in sass-loader where `prev` is showing up the wrong thing is in the `getWebpackImporter`, I think there may be something happening in webpack that is helping sass work but for whatever reason is not working for sass-embedded.

**What I am wondering:**
1) how/where does implicit resolution of nested `node_modules` folders work? (`node_modules` is mentioned in quite a lot of places in the codebase, there's nothing I spotted in the docs that goes into this level of internal detail)
2) might there be something happening that leads sass-embedded to think that the dependency that fails is called in via the other dependency rather than the main css file, but only when that other dependency is itself requiring a dependency that has already been required elsewhere? (the repo below sets up a mock of dependencies and will probably help to make sense of what this is)

Repos to demonstrate issues: 

Version with webpack, loaders and sass / sass-embedded, where sass works and sass-embedded does not for nested node_modules folders when there is a dependency called more than once elsewhere: https://github.com/debugwand/sass-embedded-bug

Version with no webpack or loaders, where neither sass nor sass-embedded works unless you list the nested node_modules folder explicitly in `includePaths` https://github.com/debugwand/sass-embedded-bug-noloaders

title: Tree-shaking fails for different imports from the same file across multiple routes
body:
### Summary

refer: [Original Issue](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/34559)
I am facing the exact issue in my production build. Since the original issue is closed and is a couple of years old, I would like to know what are the current solutions to the solution. As mentioned in the original discussion by @sokra, creating different modules for each export is difficult in my case given the shared module has inter-dependencies and thousands of exports. 

Also i would like to know why wouldn't `optimizations per runtime` work for this issue. As per the doc:
> Webpack 5 is now able (and does by default) to analyse and optimize modules per runtime (A runtime is often equal to an entrypoint). This allows to only exports in these entrypoints where they are really needed.
***
This should mean for each runtime( entry point ) if we add this optimization our next.config.js the unused exports will be re-calculated for that runtime. Hence for each page only the required code should be bundled. Please help me out understanding this optimization where i am going wrong.

### Additional information

_No response_

### Example

 https://github.com/joyt/nextjs-tree-shake-fail
 
 @alexander-akait please see this since it seems like an old problem.

title: Coffee-loader failed to process CoffeeScript files with CoffeeScriptError: error: reserved word 'var'
body:
I am trying to upgrade the Webpacker for our legacy Rails web application, which was using Webpacker V5. The goal is to upgrade from Webpacker V5 to V6 (6.0.0-rc.6) and then move to Shakapacker. However, I am stuck at coffee-loader. It seems that the CoffeeScript files are not being converted correctly.

Running `rake assets:precompile` leads to an error. I created a simple CoffeeScript file to test it, and this is the error I got:

(Note: The old Webpacker V5 configuration worked fine.)

I have tried switching coffee-loader from V1 to V5, but it didn’t help. What am I doing wrong here?

>  Module build failed (from ./node_modules/coffee-loader/dist/cjs.js):
    error: reserved word 'var'
    var hello;
    ^^^

This is my dummy coffeescript file:
```
hello = -> console.log('hi!')
```

This is my webpacker config:

```
//config/webpack/environment.js

const { webpackConfig } = require('@rails/webpacker')
const { VueLoaderPlugin } = require('vue-loader')
const vue =  require('./loaders/vue')
const coffee = require('./loaders/coffee')
const sass = require('./loaders/sass.js')
const yaml = require('./loaders/yaml.js')
const pug = require('./loaders/pug.js')
const webpack = require('webpack');

webpackConfig.plugins = [
    ...webpackConfig.plugins,
    new VueLoaderPlugin(),
    new webpack.DefinePlugin({
        __VUE_OPTIONS_API__: true,
        __VUE_PROD_DEVTOOLS__: false,
    })
]

webpackConfig.module.rules = [
    vue,
    ...webpackConfig.module.rules,
    pug,
    coffee,
    sass,
    yaml
]

delete webpackConfig.module.rules['nodeModules']

const scssConfigIndex = webpackConfig.module.rules.findIndex((config) => ".scss".match(config.test))
webpackConfig.module.rules.splice(scssConfigIndex, 1)

webpackConfig.resolve = {
  extensions: [".coffee", ".vue", ".mjs", ".js", ".sass", ".scss", ".css", ".module.sass",
    ".module.scss", ".module.css", ".png", ".svg", ".gif", ".jpeg", ".jpg"]
}

module.exports = webpackConfig

```

`loaders/coffee.js`:

```
module.exports = {
  test: /\.coffee$/,
  use: ["babel-loader", "coffee-loader"]
}
```

Here is the `package.json`:


```
{

title: Module Federation Issue: remoteEntry execute duplicated
body:
I am currently facing an issue with Module Federation. I have a hostApp that references two remote entries - app1 and app2, where app2 also references app1.

I have defined a React context in app1 but I’ve found that the context value, passed down through hostApp, is sometimes unattainable in app2. After some investigation, I’ve noticed that createContext may be executed twice, resulting in two declarations. The reason for this double execution could be that there are two instances of webpack_module_cache for app1, leading to incorrect ‘loaded: true’ module determination.

But why would the same app1 sometimes have two instances of webpack_module_cache? I’ve noticed that app1’s remoteEntry.js is also executed twice. If app1 is loaded before app2, everything functions correctly. However, if app2 is loaded before app1, this issue arises.

I’m unsure of the cause of this problem and how to resolve it. I would really appreciate some guidance on this issue. Thank you.

          }`
        );

  );
};

const jwtMiddleware = jwt({
  secret: config.get("Enterprise.encryption.jwt"),

    ) {
      return req.headers.authorization.split(" ")[1];
    }
    if (req.cookies.jwt) {
      return req.cookies.jwt;
    }
    return null;
  }
}).unless({ path: ["/login"] });

title: How to compile multiple, unrelated CSS files, in a single webpack run?
body:
In short:
I have a full-stack project, including email templates and their own styles. How to include them in webpack, when the entry is a single TSX file?

Longer version, in gulp, you can do something like this:
```
  gulp
    .src(["main.styl", "email.styl"]) // main.styl is used for the frontend part, email.styl not but needed for backend to style emails
    .pipe(
      gulpStylus(),
    ).
    .pipe(gulpRev())    
    ...
```

Like that can be injected into the app after build. Wondering if webpack can do that as well?
There are plenty of webpack hooks. Possible to compile a single, unrelated CSS separately to the main app like `email.styl` in the above example?

title: Infinite loop with custom generating plugin
body:
Hi. I'm working on a Webpack plugin for a custom language compiler -- I output a .wasm file and a .js file as part of it, using the `thisCompilation` hook. It's fairly simple, and works great, except that since it generates an `index.js` file as part of it, Webpack will keep recompiling. Verbose logging shows me the following:

```
webpack 5.91.0 compiled successfully in 836 ms
    [webpack-cli] Compiler is watching files for updates...
    [webpack-cli] File 'M:\Development\slim\_out\index.js' was modified
    [webpack-cli] Changed time is Tue Jul 30 2024 08:57:46 GMT-0700 (Pacific Daylight Time) (timestamp is 1722355066249)
    [webpack-dev-middleware] Compilation starting...
    [webpack-cli] Compiler starting... 
```

I'm wondering if there's a way to do one of the following:

1. Ignore the file from being watched in my plugin. It seems like the current ways to do that are to ask users to inject a config directly, or replace the Watcher entirely with a wrapper watcher as the WatchIgnorePlugin does; neither of which feel particularly clean.
2. Recognize which files triggered "the build", and if all of them were files that my plugin output, then do nothing in my build. Not a fan of this, since it will cause the JS bundle to get output twice, but OK.
3. Mark my files as generated output somehow.
4. Consider how to use this as a loader. My first inclination is that it doesn't fit in great with the Loader infrastructure, as the compiler takes a full directory of source files and outputs a .js and a .wasm, rather than working individually file-by-file, but I haven't investigated the loader infrastructure too deeply here.

My goal here, and the main reason I'm trying to do this as part of Webpack, is to get watch support; in all honesty, what I would like is that when one (or multiple) of my files triggers the watcher, that I recompile the full project, output a new .wasm and .js file, and then the rest of the Webpack build continues with those.

Thoughts?

When my website initialize, the two modules are both loaded. If moduleA loaded the `package1` first, the moduleB would not load `package1`, it will just use what moduleA loaded.

My question is: this behavior is Webpack feature or it is a bug.

What I expect is the two modules use their own imported `package` seperately because sometimes their code are little different.

title: Webpack 5 cache "filesystem" breaks app with default cacheFolder.
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**

I used persistent cache (cache filesystem) with DndProvider component (https://react-dnd.github.io/react-dnd/) a lot. But now got error in production build for my testing environment when using DndProvider on page while running browser test. 

```
Objects are not valid as a React child (found: object with keys {$$typeof, type, key, ref, props}). If you meant to render a collection of children, use an array instead.
```

Funny thing that i tried it on my cloud Linux dev machine and there is no problem. Now it happens only on my local machine, so the problem with cache i guess.

if cache: false, then error do not exists. I do not get the reason why it worked for a while and now even on old commits it do not work. I fully cleaned all node_modules files (.cache folder is inside node_modules) and pnpm caches, i removed fully my project and pulled it from git from scratch - did not helped. 


**Only thing that helped is setting cache_directory:**
```
{
                  type: 'filesystem',
                  cacheDirectory: path.resolve(__dirname, '.temp_cache'),
}
 ```


**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**

1. add to webpack config:
 ```
cache:  {
            type: 'filesystem',
        }
``` 
        

**What is the expected behavior?**
I expect no error to appear when we run application in testing environment (browser tests).
<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->

**Other relevant information:**
webpack version:  "5.91.0",
Node.js version: v20.13.1
Operating System: OS: macos 14.1.1

title: Is it possible to include CSS file internally within a <style> tag in the output HTML file ?
body:
this is my setting:

/src
|- index.html
|- critical.scss
|- non-critical.scss
|- index.js


my desired HTML output in /dist/index.html:


```
<html>
  <head>
  ...
    <link rel="stylesheet" href="non-critical.css"></link>
    <style>
     **css code from critical.scss**
    </style>
  </head>
  <body>
    ...
  </body>
</html>
```


 I'm currently using style-loader and MiniCssExtractPlugin in my Webpack setup. I understand that style-loader injects CSS into the DOM via JavaScript, while MiniCssExtractPlugin extracts the CSS into a separate file. Is there a way to achieve a different setup where my desired SCSS File  is directly included within the <style> tags in the HTML without relying on JavaScript.

here is my repo: https://github.com/TonyNguyen137/webpack-test

title: Path to resource is getting its backslashes removed on WIndows
body:
Hi, I've been running into a issue where it seems like a path to a resource is losing its backslashes on Windows. It seems like a classic escaping issue but attempts to work around/fix the path haven't worked out. This very well maybe a fault with something in https://github.com/devongovett/unplugin-parcel-macros but I've hit a wall debugging walking through the stack trace and would appreciate some pointers. To reproduce: repository: https://github.com/LFDanLu/s2-test-app Using Git Bash on Windows. node version 18.18.2 1. Pull the project down and install. 2. Run using yarn start. 3. Note the follow error appears: ``` ERROR in C:Usersdl164OneDriveDocumentsdevempty-projectsrcApp.js.macro-fb0d4180853462d3bcb94480fb3b2b62c62519e0b35074e4a51d1c603706c075.css Module build failed: UnhandledSchemeError: Reading from "C:Usersdl164OneDriveDocumentsdevempty-projectsrcApp.js.macro-fb0d4180853462d3bcb94480fb3b2b62c62519e0b35074e4a51d1c603706c075.css" is not handled by plugins (Unhandled scheme). Webpack supports "data:" and "file:" URIs by default. You may need an additional plugin to handle "c:" URIs. ``` Locally, I've confirmed that the output of the [unplugin-macro](https://github.com/devongovett/unplugin-parcel-macros/blob/d523d9f7c768050e83e5bedc503df75377a3d68a/unplugin-macros.js#L132) returns code that looks something like ``` code: 'import "@react-spectrum/s2/page.css";\n' + 'import { Button } from "@react-spectrum/s2";\n' + 'import React from "react";\n' + "import { hot } from 'react-hot-loader/root';\n" + 'class App extends React.Component {\n' + ' render() {\n' + ' const { name } = this.props;\n' + ' return (<>\n' + '\n' + '
awegwge
\n' + '\n' + ' \n' + '\n' + ' );\n' + ' }\n' + '}\n' + 'export default hot(App);\n' + '\n' + 'import "C:\\Users\\dl164\\OneDrive\\Documents\\dev\\empty-project\\src\\App.js.macro-fb0d4180853462d3bcb94480fb3b2b62c62519e0b35074e4a51d1c603706c075.css";', map: '{"version":3,"sources":["test.js"],"names":[],"mappings":"AAAA,OAAO,8BAA8B;AAErC,SAAS,MAAM,QAAQ,qBAAqB;AAC5C,OAAO,WAAW,QAAQ;AAC1B,SAAS,GAAG,QAAQ,wBAAwB;AAE5C,MAAM,YAAY,MAAM,SAAS;IAC/B,SAAS;QACP,MAAM,EAAE,IAAI,EAAE,GAAG,IAAI,CAAC,KAAK;QAC3B,OAAO,GACH;;MACF,CAAC,IAAI,OAAO,EAAE,IAAI;;MAClB,CAAC,OACC,iBAGD;;;;MAED,EAAE,OAAO;;MACT,GACF;IACF;AACF;AAEA,eAAe,IAAI,KAAK"}' ``` where it looks like the path is properly escaped. However, as I stepped through the stack I eventually ended up in Compilation where the request field in the [dependencies ](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/46cee287199d7dce6879aa78046fae1df9a101e6/lib/Compilation.js#L2051)shows that it has become `request: 'C:Usersdl164OneDriveDocumentsdevempty-projectsrcApp.js.macro-fb0d4180853462d3bcb94480fb3b2b62c62519e0b35074e4a51d1c603706c075.css'` at which I got stuck trying to figure out where the dependencies were coming from. I verified that modifying the path [here ](https://github.com/devongovett/unplugin-parcel-macros/blob/d523d9f7c768050e83e5bedc503df75377a3d68a/unplugin-macros.js#L78)changed what the request field became, but attempting to use posix separators or further escaping all cause it to fail at an earlier step (not super sure about this but it no longer creates a [NormalModule](https://github.com/webpack/webpack/blob/46cee287199d7dce6879aa78046fae1df9a101e6/lib/NormalModule.js#L321) with a macro path that I was previously logging against): ``` ERROR in ./src/App.js 50:0-155 Module not found: Error: Can't resolve 'C:\Users\dl164\OneDrive\Documents\dev\empty-project\src\App.js.macro-fb0d4180853462d3bcb94480fb3b2b62c62519e0b35074e4a51d1c603706c075.css' in 'C:\Users\dl164\OneDrive\Documents\dev\empty-project\src' ``` ``` Module not found: Error: Can't resolve 'C:/Users/dl164/OneDrive/Documents/dev/empty-project/src/App.js.macro-fb0d4180853462d3bcb94480fb3b2b62c62519e0b35074e4a51d1c603706c075.css' in 'C:\Users\dl164\OneDrive\Documents\dev\empty-project\src' ```

title: The project got stuck at startup
body:
hello everyone!!

I got stuck when using webpack-5 to start a project after the server started, without any error messages

Then I tried debugging and found that the program froze after entering a file at the beginning of a VM

Next, I added print information to the restore method in PackFileCacheStrategy.js and found that the program froze after a random module

``` javascript
restore(identifier, etag) {
  return this._getPack()
     .then(pack => {
        console.log('pack.requests.length >>>', pack.requests.length )
  })
}
```
As shown in the above code, when compiling to a certain file, it will suddenly get stuck, and this file is not fixed

I have tried clearing the cache, deleting node_modules, and reinstalling dependencies, but none of these issues have been resolved

This problem has been bothering me for several days, and I hope some experts can help give me some advice

Thank you very much. Great kindness and virtue will be remembered in my heart

title: webpack.SourceMapDevToolPlugin.append error -- "append can't be false when no filename is provided" ?
body:
with
```
yarn -v
	4.4.1
node -v
	v20.16.0
npm -v
	10.8.1

yarn info --name-only webpack
└─ webpack@npm:5.94.0
```

in my wp5 config, i set
```
const isProduction = process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production';
mode: isProduction ? 'production' : 'development',
...
devtool: false,
```

and, instead, use
```
new webpack.SourceMapDevToolPlugin({

	filename: isProduction ? false : 'sourcemaps/[name].[contenthash:8][ext].map',
	append: isProduction ? false : '\n//# sourceMappingURL=[url]',
	columns: true,
	module: true,
	moduleFilenameTemplate: undefined,
	fallbackModuleFilenameTemplate: undefined,
	noSources: true,
}),
```

, intended to generate only dev mode sourcemaps.

`development` mode build generates sourcemaps as intended.

`production` mode build returns

```
/srv/web/app/themes/theme01/.yarn/__virtual__/webpack-virtual-b4d35fb347/10/var/lib/wwwrun/.yarn/berry/cache/webpack-npm-5.94.0-d1e43de389-10c0.zip/node_modules/webpack/lib/SourceMapDevToolPlugin.js:551

Error: SourceMapDevToolPlugin: append can't be false when no filename is provided
    at /srv/web/app/themes/theme01/.yarn/__virtual__/webpack-virtual-b4d35fb347/10/var/lib/wwwrun/.yarn/berry/cache/webpack-npm-5.94.0-d1e43de389-10c0.zip/node_modules/webpack/lib/SourceMapDevToolPlugin.js:551:18
    at arrayEach (/var/lib/wwwrun/.yarn/berry/cache/neo-async-npm-2.6.2-75d6902586-10c0.zip/node_modules/neo-async/async.js:2405:9)
    at Object.each (/var/lib/wwwrun/.yarn/berry/cache/neo-async-npm-2.6.2-75d6902586-10c0.zip/node_modules/neo-async/async.js:2846:9)
    at /srv/web/app/themes/theme01/.yarn/__virtual__/webpack-virtual-b4d35fb347/10/var/lib/wwwrun/.yarn/berry/cache/webpack-npm-5.94.0-d1e43de389-10c0.zip/node_modules/webpack/lib/SourceMapDevToolPlugin.js:419:17
    at /var/lib/wwwrun/.yarn/berry/cache/neo-async-npm-2.6.2-75d6902586-10c0.zip/node_modules/neo-async/async.js:2830:7
    at done (/var/lib/wwwrun/.yarn/berry/cache/neo-async-npm-2.6.2-75d6902586-10c0.zip/node_modules/neo-async/async.js:2865:11)
    at /var/lib/wwwrun/.yarn/berry/cache/neo-async-npm-2.6.2-75d6902586-10c0.zip/node_modules/neo-async/async.js:2818:7
    at /srv/web/app/themes/theme01/.yarn/__virtual__/webpack-virtual-b4d35fb347/10/var/lib/wwwrun/.yarn/berry/cache/webpack-npm-5.94.0-d1e43de389-10c0.zip/node_modules/webpack/lib/SourceMapDevToolPlugin.js:350:9
    at /srv/web/app/themes/theme01/.yarn/__virtual__/webpack-virtual-b4d35fb347/10/var/lib/wwwrun/.yarn/berry/cache/webpack-npm-5.94.0-d1e43de389-10c0.zip/node_modules/webpack/lib/Cache.js:97:5
    at Hook.eval [as callAsync] (eval at create (/var/lib/wwwrun/.yarn/berry/cache/tapable-npm-2.2.1-8cf5ff3039-10c0.zip/node_modules/tapable/lib/HookCodeFactory.js:33:10), <anonymous>:6:1)

Node.js v20.16.0
```

unclear if this is a config issue, or a bug.

how to

	(1) only generate sourcemap in dev mode,

AND

	(2) append only in dev mode

?
author: pgnd

title: wp5+mini-css-extract-plugin build bundles imported vendor scss (bootstrap5), but into `main.css`, not specific `vendor.css`?
body:
i'm building with webpack5

i use yarn4+pnp
```
yarn -v
	4.4.1
```

i've yarn-installed bootstrap5

i import bootstrap scss into a single entry point

wp splitChunks handles code split of bootstrap JS to a separate `boostrap.js`

i'm using MiniCssExtractPlugin to bundle AND split off bootstrap's CSS, into a separate `bootstrap.css`
```
new MiniCssExtractPlugin({
	filename: isProduction ? '[name].[contenthash].css' : '[name].css',
}),
```

after webpack build, no errors, i get
```
tree build
	build/
	├── js
	│   └── bootstrap.js
	├── main.css
	├── main.js
	├── runtime_manifest.js
	└── sourcemaps
	    ├── main.css.map
	    └── main.js.map

ls -1s build/main.*
	268K build/main.css
	4.0K build/main.js

grep -rlni bootstrap build/
	build/main.css
	build/js/bootstrap.js

cat build/main.css
	/*!***********************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************!*\
	  !*** css ./.yarn/__virtual__/css-loader-virtual-2a44455ff5/5/var/lib/wwwrun/.yarn/berry/cache/css-loader-npm-7.1.2-7540f12884-10c0.zip/node_modules/css-loader/dist/cjs.js??ruleSet[1].rules[1].use[1]!./.yarn/__virtual__/sass-loader-virtual-e45d913bf0/5/var/lib/wwwrun/.yarn/berry/cache/sass-loader-npm-16.0.1-0d9dc804c0-10c0.zip/node_modules/sass-loader/dist/cjs.js??ruleSet[1].rules[1].use[2]!./main.scss ***!
	  \***********************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************************/
	@charset "UTF-8";
	/*!
	 * Bootstrap  v5.3.3 (https://getbootstrap.com/)
	 * Copyright 2011-2024 The Bootstrap Authors
	 * Licensed under MIT (https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/blob/main/LICENSE)
	 */
	:root,
	[data-bs-theme=light] {
	...
```

i.e., boostrap.css *IS* bundled, but not into the intended target, `bootstrap.css`.
rather, into the main css 'chunk', `main.css`

is my config/usage incorrect? either specific to MiniCssExtractPlugin, or generally?
what needs to change to get that `bootstrap.css` correctly split off and populated?
or, is this a known issue/bug?


here's a reproducer,
```
git clone https://codeberg.org/pgnd-test/wp5test TEST
cd TEST
yarn
yarn d
tree build
	build/
	├── js
	│   └── bootstrap.js
	├── main.css
	├── main.js
	├── runtime_manifest.js
	└── sourcemaps
	    ├── main.css.map
	    └── main.js.map
```
author: pgnd

title: How to define chunk names in 3rd party code?
body:
Sometimes we encounter 3rd party code that contains async imports, which will cause webpack to create a new chunk group.  If there is no `webpackChunkName` magic comment defined, the chunk group will be anonymous and the final chunk name will be generic.  Furthermore, each async import creates a separate chunk group, leading (potentially) to many small network requests.

I believe the only way to control the chunk group definitions is via the `webpackChunkName` magic comments.  But what is the recommendation for 3rd part code?  Is there a good way to inject magic comments into such code at build time?

I found this open source project that uses a loader:
https://github.com/morganney/magic-comments-loader

That's an option but it looks like it parses each module again so I was hoping there might be a more "built-in" alternative approach that leverages the parsing already done by webpack itself.

Any ideas?

title: Dynamic Module Federation: singleton library instantiated 2x
body:
In a project with a host and a remote app, I noticed that the npm library I'm developing (it's a framework which is used both by host and remote apps), is bundled by the remote as well. I tried all the various settings, etc, to no avail. If I set a debug breakpoint I can see that Singleton classes in this library instantiated 2x first by the host app, and then by the remote. 

My issue is the same as described here: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/65467233/wp5-module-federation-singleton-instantiated-multiple-times?rq=2

Hoping someone can point me towards some documentation or better yet, working example. (I did look at for example the repo with tons of federation examples https://github.com/module-federation/module-federation-examples however couldn't find any solution to the problem there.

title: In module federation, is there a way to know whether the current module is being used by the host application or by a remote application?

# Bug report

**What is the current behavior?**

``` js
const path = require("path");
const HtmlWebpackPlugin = require("html-webpack-plugin");
const { ModuleFederationPlugin } = require("webpack").container;
const rules = [
	{
		test: /\.js$/,
		include: path.resolve(__dirname, "src"),
		use: {
			loader: "babel-loader",
			options: {
				presets: ["@babel/react"]
			}
		}
	}
];
const getConfig = (env = "development") => {
	env = "development";
	return [
		{
			name: "app",
			context: __dirname,
			mode: env,
			devtool: "source-map",
			entry: {
				app: "./src/index.js"
			},
			output: {
				filename: "[name].js",
				path: path.resolve(__dirname, "dist/aaa"),
				uniqueName: "module-federation-aaa"
			},
			module: { rules },
			optimization: {
				runtimeChunk: "single",
				splitChunks: {
					chunks: "all",
					cacheGroups: {
						efg:{
							// react
							test: /[\\/]node_modules[\\/]react[\\/]/,
							name: "npm.react",
							priority: 10
						}
					},
				},
			},
			plugins: [
				new ModuleFederationPlugin({
					remoteType: "script", 
					name: "mfeAAA",
					library: {
						type: "umd",
						name: "mfeAAA"
					},
					runtime: false,
					// List of remotes with URLs
					remotes: {
						"mfe-a": "mfeAAA@/mfeAAA.js"
					},
					exposes: {
						"./Component": "./src/D"
					},
				}),
				new HtmlWebpackPlugin({
					template: "./index.html"
				})
			],
		},
	]
};
module.exports = getConfig;

```



This is an example of using module federation for self-use.

This webpack configuration will output `app.js`, `runtime.js`, `mfeAAA.js`, and other unimportant JS files. The order they are included in `index.html` is `runtime.js`, `app.js`, `mfeAAA.js`.

The key points of this issue are:
- webpack has `runtime: 'single'`, and `ModuleFederationPlugin` has option `runtime: false`.
- Both `runtime.js` and `mfeAAA.js` internally register `webpackJsonpCallback`.
- When `app.js` loaded, there is a callback `__webpack_require__.O(0, ["npm.react","vendors......`,
```
__webpack_require__ => { // webpackRuntimeModules
var __webpack_exec__ = (moduleId) => (__webpack_require__(__webpack_require__.s = moduleId))
  __webpack_require__.O(0, ["npm.react","vendors-node_modules_pnpm_react-dom_18_3_1_react_18_3_1_node_modules_react-dom_index_js"], () => (__webpack_exec__("./src/index.js")));
var __webpack_exports__ = __webpack_require__.O();
}
```
- But here, the `__webpack_require__` is from inside `mfeAAA.js`, and it does not define `__webpack_require__.O



**What is the expected behavior?**

<!-- "It should work" is not a helpful explanation -->
<!-- Explain exactly how it should behave -->



**Other relevant information:**

webpack version:  5.95.0
Node.js version:
Operating System:
Additional tools:

**Why would use self module federation?**

In some micro-front-end scenarios，appA will be embedded in appB, and appB will use the module federation of appA

The above example is not realistic; module federation in Webpack will not have a configuration that uses itself. I'm referring to the situation where mfeAAA.js and runtime.js are loaded together at runtime

title: removeAvailableModules
body:
removeAvailableModules 这个配置项的作用需要怎么搭建例子才能看到效果呢，我的理解是如下

util.js 共享模块
parent.js   动态 imoprt 引入 children.js  引入 util.js
children.js    引入 util.js

如果 removeAvailableModules :false 那么 util.js 会被打包在 parent.js 和 动态 import 分割出的模块中,
如果 removeAvailableModules :true 那么 util.js 只会被打包在 parent.js 中  因为对于 children.js , parent.js 是 父模块,而父模块中包含了子模块 children.js 需要导入的模块，所以只有一个结果

但打包后并不是这样。是我理解错了吗？

title: using HTML-Webpack-plugin to use multiple HTML files, but one of them cannot execute JS script?
body:
Hello, this is probably is a newbie question as I'm sure the answer is very blatant, but I've been at it for hours and I can't figure it out.

I'm using webpack to compile a project using the BabylonJS framework for rendering 3D simulations. The main files are here: 
#https://github.com/ClemGG/filedump/tree/main/babylonsxd

In the webpack.config.js file, I create multiple htmlPlugins that I then pass to the plugins options of the file. Running ` npm build run` successfully produces the bundle files and the copies of the html files to the dist folder.

Opening the index file in localhost, things seem to run just fine, but when opening the editor page by pressing enter, the script referenced in editor.html (dist/editor.bundles.js) doesn't seem to get called. The terminal shows no compilation errors, and neither the console nor the network windows throw any exception. I've checked if the issue came from the HTML side, but everything seems to be in order. It's like the file doesn't even recognize the <script> tag in both the body and the head of the editor.html file, while it does in the index.html file.

I have no idea what could be causing this. Any help would be welcome. Thank you for your time.

title: webpack contenthash  chunkhash  chunk
body:
对于 contenthash 和 chunkhash 上网查阅的资料显示，一个相对于文件内容，一个相对于 chunk 内容，但是 对于某一个 chunk 中，如果分离出去一些文件（例如 通过 MiniCssExtractPlugin 分离出去的 css） 这些 css 文件的 chunkhash 是相对于被切割的 chunk 的，但是如果分离出去的 文件 是 js ，这个文件则自己成为了 一个 chunk ，chunkhash 则相对于自己 ，对于 图片，chunkhash 将无效，只有 contenthash 生效 这让我非常困惑，希望有人能解答


For contenthash and chunkhash The data consulted on the Internet shows that one is relative to the content of the file and the other is relative to the content of the chunk, but For a chunk, if some files are separated out (e.g. css separated by MiniCssExtractPlugin) The chunkhash of these css files is relative to the chunk being cut, but if the separated file is js, This file becomes a chunk on its own, and chunkhash is relative to itself, for images, chunkhash will be invalid, only contenthash will work, which is very confusing to me, I hope someone can answer

title: New very slow build time with webpack 5.96.0
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

Hi team, I just bumped my webpack from 5.95.0 to the newly released 5.96.0 and this has resulted in increased build times from 2-3minutes to ~15minutes for my React SPA. I first noticed this in my automated deployment pipelines in Bitbucket, but the same thing is noticed on my local dev machine. 

I downgraded back to 5.95.0 and the builds returned to the usual times, so definitely something to do with 5.96.0. 

Happy to help with debugging this - please let me know how to setup my environment to provide you with required logs. 

webpack.config.ts:

```
import webpack from 'webpack'
import { relative, resolve } from 'path'
import HtmlWebpackPlugin from 'html-webpack-plugin'
import MiniCssExtractPlugin from 'mini-css-extract-plugin'
import CssMinimizerPlugin from 'css-minimizer-webpack-plugin'
import TerserPlugin from 'terser-webpack-plugin'
import CopyWebpackPlugin from 'copy-webpack-plugin'
import ESLintPlugin from 'eslint-webpack-plugin'
import ReactRefreshWebpackPlugin from '@pmmmwh/react-refresh-webpack-plugin'
import ReactRefreshTypeScript from 'react-refresh-typescript'
import ForkTsCheckerWebpackPlugin from 'fork-ts-checker-webpack-plugin'
import { InjectManifest } from 'workbox-webpack-plugin'
import { getPublicUrlOrPath } from './src/utilities/getPublicUrlOrPath'
import { WebpackManifestPlugin } from 'webpack-manifest-plugin'

module.exports = (env: { APP_BUILD?: string; FUNCTION_APP?: string }) => {
  //get PUBLIC_URL, which is needed for production builds where process (which is a Node server var), doesn't exist
  const publicUrlOrPath = getPublicUrlOrPath(process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development', undefined, process.env.PUBLIC_URL)

  //set whether are creating source maps with prod builds
  // eslint-disable-next-line @typescript-eslint/no-inferrable-types
  const genSourceMaps: boolean = true

  const prod = process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production'

  return {
    mode: prod ? 'production' : 'development',
    cache: { type: 'filesystem' },
    infrastructureLogging: { level: 'info' },
    stats: 'normal',
    // eslint-disable-next-line @typescript-eslint/no-unnecessary-condition
    devtool: !prod ? (genSourceMaps ? 'source-map' : false) : false, //https://webpack.js.org/configuration/devtool/
    bail: false, //this needs to be set to false for dev builds otherwise dev-server will break every time an import can't be resolved
    resolve: {
      modules: [resolve(__dirname, 'node_modules'), resolve(__dirname, './src')]
    },
    entry: './src/index.tsx',
    output: {
      path: resolve(__dirname, 'build'),
      publicPath: publicUrlOrPath,
      filename: prod ? '[name].[contenthash:8].js' : undefined,
      chunkFilename: prod ? '[name].[contenthash:8].chunk.js' : undefined,
      assetModuleFilename: 'media/[name].[hash][ext]',
      clean: true, //clears the output dir prior to building

      // Point sourcemap entries to original disk location (format as URL on Windows)
      // eslint-disable-next-line @typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any
      devtoolModuleFilenameTemplate: (info: any) => {
        // eslint-disable-next-line @typescript-eslint/no-unsafe-member-access
        if (prod) return relative('./src', info.absoluteResourcePath).replace(/\\/g, '/')
        // eslint-disable-next-line @typescript-eslint/no-unsafe-member-access
        else return resolve(info.absoluteResourcePath).replace(/\\/g, '/')
      }
    },
    devServer: {
      port: '3000',
      static: ['./public'],
      open: true,
      hot: true,
      historyApiFallback: true,
      client: {
        overlay: {
          errors: true,
          warnings: false,
          runtimeErrors: true
        }
      },
      devMiddleware: {
        writeToDisk: true //massively speeds up loading of dev server
      }
    },
    module: {
      rules: [
        //Typescript loader
        //NOTE: uses fork-ts-checker-webpack-plugin for TS checking in dev on a separate thread
        {
          test: /\.([cm]?ts|tsx)$/,
          exclude: /node_modules/,
          resolve: {
            extensions: ['.ts', '.tsx', '.js'] // --> JSON and HTML gets parsed by webpacks internal loaders
          },

          use: [
            {
              loader: require.resolve('ts-loader'),
              options: {
                getCustomTransformers: () => ({
                  before: [!prod && ReactRefreshTypeScript()].filter(Boolean)
                }),
                transpileOnly: !prod
              }
            }
          ]
        },

        //CSS loaders
        {
          test: /\.css$/,
          exclude: /node_modules/,
          use: [
            //In prod, MiniCSSExtractPlugin extract CSS to file(s), but in development "style" loader enables hot editing of CSS.
            prod && MiniCssExtractPlugin.loader,

            //style loader turns CSS into JS modules that inject <style> tags
            !prod && require.resolve('style-loader'),

            //css-loader resolves paths in CSS and adds assets as dependencies
            {
              loader: require.resolve('css-loader'),
              options: { sourceMap: genSourceMaps }
            },

            //postcss loader applies autoprefixer to CSS
            {
              loader: require.resolve('postcss-loader'),
              options: { sourceMap: genSourceMaps }
            }
          ].filter(Boolean),
          sideEffects: true //See https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/6571
        },

        //File loader (including images)
        {
          exclude: /node_modules/,
          test: /\.(png|jp(e*)g|svg|gif|ttf)$/,
          type: 'asset/resource'
        }
      ].filter(Boolean)
    },
    plugins: [
      //Generates an `index.html` file with the <script> injected.
      new HtmlWebpackPlugin({ filename: 'index.html', template: 'index.html' }),

      //Copies the public directory into the root of build directory
      new CopyWebpackPlugin({ patterns: [{ from: 'public' }] }),

      //Makes environment variables available to the build code
      new webpack.DefinePlugin({
        'process.env': {
          NODE_ENV: JSON.stringify(process.env.NODE_ENV),
          PUBLIC_URL: JSON.stringify(publicUrlOrPath.slice(0, -1)),
          APP_BUILD: JSON.stringify(env.APP_BUILD),
          FUNCTION_APP: JSON.stringify(env.FUNCTION_APP)
        }
      }),

      ...(prod
        ? [
            //Extracts CSS into separate files
            new MiniCssExtractPlugin({
              filename: '[name].[contenthash:8].css',
              chunkFilename: '[name].[contenthash:8].chunk.css'
            }),

            //Generate a service worker script that will precache, and keep up to date, the HTML & assets that are part of the webpack build
            new InjectManifest({
              swSrc: './src/service-worker.ts',
              dontCacheBustURLsMatching: /\.[0-9a-f]{8}\./,
              exclude: [/\.map$/, /asset-manifest\.json$/, /LICENSE/],
              maximumFileSizeToCacheInBytes: 5 * 1024 * 1024 //5MB currently to cache large builds, but should split and lazy-load
            }),

            // Generate an asset manifest file
            new WebpackManifestPlugin({
              fileName: 'asset-manifest.json',
              publicPath: publicUrlOrPath,
              generate: (seed, files, entrypoints) => {
                const manifestFiles = files.reduce((manifest, file) => {
                  manifest[file.name] = file.path
                  return manifest
                }, seed)

                const entrypointFiles = entrypoints.main.filter(fileName => !fileName.endsWith('.map'))

                return {
                  files: manifestFiles,
                  entrypoints: entrypointFiles
                }
              }
            })
          ]
        : []),

      ...(!prod
        ? [
            //Checks code for issues with eslint
            new ESLintPlugin({ extensions: ['js', 'mjs', 'jsx', 'ts', 'tsx'] }),

            //Enables new React hot reloading in dev builds
            new ReactRefreshWebpackPlugin(),

            //Used for TS checking - does the checks on a separate process to the build
            //NOTE: This plugin uses TypeScript's, not Webpack's modules resolution (i.e. uses tsconfig.json)
            new ForkTsCheckerWebpackPlugin()
          ]
        : [])
    ],
    optimization: {
      nodeEnv: false, //do not modify/set the value of process.env.NODE_ENV as this is done by DefinePlugin
      minimizer: [
        //minimise JS
        new TerserPlugin({
          extractComments: false,
          terserOptions: {
            format: {
              comments: false
            }
          }
        }),

        //minimise CSS
        new CssMinimizerPlugin()
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

title: How to import modules within plugins and avoid MODULE_NOT_FOUND from the webpack-dev-middleware
body:
I'm writing a webpack plugin that imports a module, based on a file path, similar to:
```js
const config = (await import(path)).default();
```
This isn't a file that is otherwise imported into the compilation as a module.

This works fine during build, and using HMR without `webpack-dev-middleware`

However, despite the `inputFileSystem` recognising the file path, `MODULE_NOT_FOUND` is thrown from the `webpack-dev-middleware` compile, when attempting the above `import`.

I've tried adding a file dependency to the compilation, to various modules at various places during build, creating loaders that add a dependency, all to no avail.

Has anyone got experience of importing a module from disk during a compilation and having `webpack-dev-middleware` not complain?
author: TomStrepsil

title: What is the right compilation hook to transform asset module names prior to emit?
body:
Our CDN has a limitation where all uploaded files need to have lowercase names so that they are fetched correctly. For chunks, we have a custom Webpack plugin that taps into `afterChunks`/`afterOptimizeChunks` and transforms chunk names into lowercase by setting `chunk.name = chunk.name.toLowerCase();`, which ensures that resulting chunk filenames are compatible with the CDN. 

Now we are switching our static assets (images and fonts) from `file-loader` to `asset/resource` asset modules and need to have the same transformation applied to them as well. What would be the right hook to transform file names of these assets (either a new one specific to asset modules or a generic one that could transform names for both chunks and asset modules)?

Currently we have a workaround by setting a transform function to `output.assetModuleFilename`, but we would rather have a (custom) plugin to be responsible for this. Thanks! 🙂

title: Is it possible to only use filesystem caching for builds that aren't re-builds?
body:
**Is it possible to only use filesystem caching for builds that aren't re-builds?**

I recently upgraded a project to use Webpack 5.95.0 and am running into performance issues with my development builds. Without filesystem caching on, build times are significantly (>50% slower) than on Webpack 4. Turning on filesystem caching alleviates some of this pain by reducing build times when the cache is warm such that the average build time is reasonable.

However, in development (watch mode on), I'm observing higher recompile times when filesystem caching is turned on. I'm suspecting this is because time is spent dealing with the filesystem caching, though I'm not sure.

I'd like to keep benefitting from the filesystem cache on full builds, but I want to avoid writing or reading from the cache in watch mode. Then just write to the cache when shutting down webpack. Is this possible?

**What is the current behavior?**
All variables exported from an ESM that contains a top-level awaited dynamic import are undefined.
**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
```js
if(isElectron){
   var { IPC } = await import('../../ENV/electron')
}
export const something = () => {
   IPC?.on('json',() => {...})
}
```
This case demonstrates that some logic depends on a dynamic module, and `something` is provided by ProvidePlugin, but it's `undefined` at runtime. This issue can be resolved by replacing `await import()` with `require()`, but this makes the ESM code impure.

title: How to bundle Emscripten output (ES6 module) with webpack?
body:
Hello,
I'm trying to understand how to bundle an app with webpack.
This app depends on a library (of mine) which in turns depends on .js+.wasm files coming from Emscripten compilation.

My project structure is the following:
```
./node_modules/library/
- module.mjs
- module.wasm
- index.mjs

./app/
- index.mjs --> depends on ../node_modules/library/index.mjs
- webpack.config.cjs
```

The module.mjs and module.wasm are Emscripten's output and are compiled with `MODULARIZE=1`, `EXPORT_ES6=1`.

The setup I've given to `library` is good for me as it enables the user to depend on `library` by using ES6 imports.

However, when the library is used on the web, for several reasons I would like to bundle the library using webpack.

I don't have preference over the output (one file vs multiple files), even having one single `bundle.js` file would be fine, just I'd like to see it working.

I'm facing several issues:
- emscripten puts node/web/worker code into one file. webpack tries to follow imports toward node packages (e.g. process) and fails. I've managed to avoid this using the `webpack.IgnorePlugin` to avoid following any import arose from `module.mjs`.\
Now the output is `bundle.js`, `js_lib_module-wasm_js.bundle.js`, `<hash>.wasm`.
- Now, almost everything is working, until `module.mjs` doesn't try to start the web worker. I've discovered webpack replaces `import.meta.url` with the path of the original file (e.g. `file://<parent>/node_modules/library/module.mjs`), and **not the webpack's output bundle**, and thus the call to `new Worker(new URL("file://<parent>/node_modules/library/module.mjs"))` fails.

I'm asking for any quick fix to this issue, but most importantly: isn't there a working example of app + ES6 Emscripten module? 
Thanks,

author: danehansen

title: Help understanding meaning of webpack chunk loading global state and empty chunks
body: Hello, I'm looking at the global variable webpack uses for chunk loading and I'm hoping somebody can help me understand what I'm seeing. The variable I'm referring to is the one whose name can (optionally) be defined by [output.chunkLoadingGlobal](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/output/#outputchunkloadingglobal). There is an element in this global array for every chunk that is loaded. Inside each element are 2 more arrays. The first array to identify the chunk. The second array to list all of the modules that live inside that chunk. Normally, the "chunk id" array contains only a single element, with the name of the chunk in it. But I found quite a number of cases in my build where the "chunk id" array has multiple elements, with each one referring to a different chunk. For example, this one has 9 elements: If I open the chunk from the first element, `fieldSelection` I see that it indeed has this same 9 element array in it , and it contains all of the listed modules: My first question is: what does it mean when a chunk has a list of other chunks in its id? I'm guessing it means that all of these chunks were found to share the same list of modules? Now, if I look at the build output on disk, I also find the other 8 chunks also exist (although they are not loaded into the browser at runtime). If I open them, I see they are all basically empty. They all look like this: Here you can see the chunk id list contains 8 elements instead of 9 elements. The missing element is the `fieldSelection` one from above that had the actual modules in it. All of the 8 changes are like this, with the 8 element chunk ids (differing only by order) and a single empty function with this same name `"?13bc"`. Does anybody know why these nearly empty stump-chunks are being generated? What is the empty function for? In my webpack config I have `removeEmptyChunks` set to true. Are these not considered empty chunks? That `"?13bc"` function actually exists in quite a lot of my chunks, empty or not. If anybody can shed some light on what this all means, I would really appreciate it. I'm trying to reduce the size of my build and runtime footprint by reducing redundancy and duplicated modules, so understanding how this works will help me make progress in figuring the rest of that out.
author: scameron

title: Strategies for reducing/eliminating module duplication across chunks
body:
I've never been able to understand the exact reason why, but I often see modules duplicated across multiple chunks in our build.  I know splitChunks tries to split out common modules into separate chunks that get shared between chunk groups, but for reasons that are mysterious to me, it doesn't always do this.

Typically, the duplicated modules are modules that are statically imported by code in multiple async loaded chunk groups.  Not all of the common modules are duplicated into each chunk group, but a significant number are.

If we assume in a complex enough codebase splitChunks can't automatically do a perfect job of deduping, what else can be done?  I've played around a bit with cache groups but I've never been able to get a satisfactory outcome.  Explicitly referencing individual modules seems brittle because code changes over time.  And pulling in entire folders or directories can end up adding too much to a cache group, subverting the normal build optimizations.  Maybe there's a way to find the sweet spot here, but I haven't found it yet.

Does anybody have any good strategies for analyzing duplication and eliminating it?  If anybody has been able to get a good outcome in a reasonably large/complex code base, I'd be super interested in any ideas or hints you might have in mind.

Thanks in advance!
author: scameron

title: Webpack is emitting incorrect sourcemaps for Sass
body: So, I've been tearing my hair out over this problem with Webpack generating incorrect sourcemaps for my Sass files. When I inspect in developer tools (or use a tool like [this one](https://evanw.github.io/source-map-visualization/)), the generated CSS maps to the wrong source files. This only seems to happen with my own code, not third-party Sass files (I'm including Bootstrap in my project). I've definitely narrowed the issue down to being Webpack-related: When I compile from the command line with (Dart) Sass, the emitted sourcemap is correct. I've setup [an example project](https://github.com/rhaglennydd/my-sass-webpack-project) to prove this. The Webpack configuration there is nearly identical to my actual project's config (except the actual project's config also compiles JS, images, fonts, etc.). Compare the output in the dist directory when you run `npm run build` to when you run `sass --embed-sources -I ./node_modules/ src/scss/app.scss:dist/app.css`, there's definitely a difference in the main sourcemap. If it matters, you'll notice that I'm using `@use` directives instead of `@import` (as opposed to Bootstrap's outdated Sass code).

The webpack config of parent app has the following rule ``` { test: /routes\.js$/, use: [ { loader: path.resolve("routeloader.js"), options: {}, }, ], } ``` The `routeloader.js` imports the child app routes and injects in the parent app routes. author: erosman

title: Should JSON loader preserve the __proto__ property?
body:
Webpack native support JSON file. When import JSON file, we treat it as js object and parsed by JSON.parse. But `__proto__`  behaves differently in [`object_literal_syntax_vs` and `.json`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/Object_initializer#object_literal_syntax_vs._json).

> In JSON, "__proto__" is a normal property key. In an object literal, it [sets the object's prototype](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/Object_initializer#prototype_setter).

So my idea is If we treat it as a js object, should we handle `__proto__` key specially?

Related: https://github.com/evanw/esbuild/issues/3700
author: hai-x

title: How to setup the pure ES modules loading with "webpack-node-externals"
body:
I am asking this question after [has not been answered to similar one in Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/q/79259711/4818123) despite the fact that I have started the bounty and created the [reproduction](https://github.com/TokugawaTakeshi/Issue-WebpackNodeExternalsAndESM). Hope this question will be answered here.

---

## Remark

Weird things are happening with modules types is Node.js.
On the one hand, the CommonJS modules are still (for the end of 2024) *default* in Node.js.
On the other hand, the [agitating about migrating to ECMAScript modules][2] is in process. 

However, this migration is not simple in usually will not ends by replacing of `require()` to `import` and adding of `"type": "module"` to the **package.json**. So, because a large number of npm libraries are still CommonJS, installing the various decencies to our project usually we have the mashup of CommonJS modules and ECMAScript modules:

[![enter image description here][3]][3]

In [gulp-imagemin][4], there is the issue titled [8.0.0 - ESM only? Serious?][5].
Maybe it is outdated (Aug 2021), but I still facing with ESM incompatibility errors using all Webpack, **tsx** and **ts-node**. Well, this question about **Webpack**, so time to return to the subject of this question.

## My Case

I am creating the project building tool, but to build its own source code, the Webpack is being used.

[![enter image description here][6]][6]

AFAIK:

* The Webpack is partially ignores the **tsconfig.json** in favor to the Webpack configuration.
Particularly, the [`target: "node"`](https://webpack.js.org/configuration/target/) affects on which modules will be at the output.
* The usage of [webpack-node-externals][7] is de facto required for Node.js because without it the plenty of warning and/or errors will be on trying to use some native and/or npm modules.

So, if to make the previous scheme more detailed, besides the output entry point there are will be the plenty of non-bundled node_modules:

[![enter image description here][8]][8]

The building of entry point going well, but if to try to execute the built entry point, the error like the following one will be:

```Back
D:\IntelliJ IDEA\InHouseDevelopment\Example\MainPackage\EntryPoint.js:14040
module.exports = require("gulp-imagemin");
                 ^

Error [ERR_REQUIRE_ESM]: require() of ES Module D:\IntelliJ IDEA\InHouseDevelopment\Example\MainPackage\node_modules\gulp-imagemin\index.js from D:\IntelliJ IDEA\InHouseDevelopment\Example\MainPackage\EntryPoint.js not supported.
Instead change the require of index.js in D:\IntelliJ IDEA\InHouseDevelopment\Example\MainPackage\EntryPoint.js to a dynamic import() which is available in all CommonJS modules.
    at gulp-imagemin (D:\IntelliJ IDEA\InHouseDevelopment\Example\MainPackage\EntryPoint.js:14040:18)
    at __webpack_require__ (D:\IntelliJ IDEA\InHouseDevelopment\Example\MainPackage\EntryPoint.js:14370:42)
    at ./ProjectBuilding/AssetsProcessing/Images/ImagesProcessor.ts (D:\IntelliJ IDEA\InHouseDevelopment\Example\MainPackage\EntryPoint.js:1093:41)
    at __webpack_require__ (D:\IntelliJ IDEA\InHouseDevelopment\Example\MainPackage\EntryPoint.js:14370:42)
    at ./ProjectBuilder.ts (D:\IntelliJ IDEA\InHouseDevelopment\Example\MainPackage\EntryPoint.js:166:43) {
  code: 'ERR_REQUIRE_ESM'
}

Node.js v22.0.0
```

Some specific packages. 

* The [gulp-imagemin][4] is being used directly. From the version 8.0.0 it is the pure ESM.
* The [imagemin][9] on which the previous one depends also the ESM since 8.0.0
* Since version 1.0.0, [w3c-html-validator][10] is not compatible with my Webpack preset. In the middle of 2024, the error has been caused by the [chalk][11] dependency of **w3c-html-validator**. Currently, with version 1.8.2, **w3c-html-validator** itself causes the "Error [ERR_REQUIRE_ESM]: require() of ES Module" error.


## Reproduction

The [reproduction][12] includes the cases with **imagemin** + **imagemin-pngquant** and **w3c-html-validator**.

If you will be so kind to checkout this GitHub repository, all you need to get the above error is execute

```bash
node ImageminPngquantTest.js
```

for **imagemin** + **imagemin-pngquant** test, and 

```bash
node HTMLValidationTest.js 
```

for the **w3c-html-validator**. In both cases, you will get the "Error [ERR_REQUIRE_ESM]: require() of ES Module" error.


If you want to correct the source TypeScript code, please execute

```bash
npm run build
```

to rebuild the output JavaScript code.


## Running away from the problems

The following measures are the workarounds rather than solution.

### Use the dynamic import

Depending on the application, it could cause the large-scale refactoring.
For example, assume that previously the functionality imported synchronously is being used in the classes' constructors. But the class constructor could not be async. Thus, we must do something, for example, split the initialization to 2 steps.

For the Gulp case, most of Gulp task functions (especially the ones which are using the plugins) returns the streams, not promises. Unfortunately, the combining of promises and streams is not simple in the Gulp.

Well, even if above problems are solvable, it was just the example. But generally, the starting of the usage of the dynamic import could cause the large-scale changes in the code.


### Set `type: module` to the package.json

I can not understand hot it could be recommended such simply.

**The adding of `type: module` to "package.json" could and most likely will brake both project and what depends on it!**.

So, unfortunately it is not that could be simply done.


  [1]: https://www.npmjs.com/package/webpack-node-externals
  [2]: https://gist.github.com/sindresorhus/a39789f98801d908bbc7ff3ecc99d99c
  [3]: https://i.sstatic.net/4aMj8HPL.png
  [4]: https://github.com/sindresorhus/gulp-imagemin
  [5]: https://github.com/sindresorhus/gulp-imagemin/issues/355
  [6]: https://i.sstatic.net/82I6UwoT.png
  [7]: https://github.com/liady/webpack-node-externals
  [8]: https://i.sstatic.net/Yj540Ivx.png
  [9]: https://github.com/imagemin/imagemin
  [10]: https://github.com/center-key/w3c-html-validator
  [11]: https://github.com/chalk/chalk
  [12]: https://github.com/TokugawaTakeshi/Issue-WebpackNodeExternalsAndESM

title: [harmony import] Failed to add property to namespace when the module value is a function
body:
# Bug report **What is the current behavior?** ``` target: node12.20 format: commonjs externalsPresets: { node: false, }, resolve: { fallback:{ stream: require.resove("stream-browserify") } } ``` ```ts // source code import * as Stream from 'stream' const { Writable } = Stream new Writable() ``` In my case, it was processed like: ```ts // EXTERNAL MODULE: ./node_modules/@ali/stream-browserify/index.js var stream_browserify = __webpack_require__(45367); // stream_browserify is function Stream() { // EE.call(this); // } var stream_browserify_namespaceObject = /*#__PURE__*/__webpack_require__.t(stream_browserify, 2); const { Writable } = stream_browserify_namespaceObject; new Writable() // Error ! Writeable is undefined, cause it should be access from `stream_browserify_namespaceObject.default` ``` I notice there're two webpack runtime helper code `__webpack_require__.n` and `__webpack_require__.t`:
the helper function source code

```js /* webpack/runtime/compat get default export */ /******/ (() => { /******/ // getDefaultExport function for compatibility with non-harmony modules /******/ __webpack_require__.n = (module) => { /******/ var getter = module && module.__esModule ? /******/ () => (module['default']) : /******/ () => (module); /******/ __webpack_require__.d(getter, { a: getter }); /******/ return getter; /******/ }; /******/ })(); /* webpack/runtime/create fake namespace object */ /******/ (() => { /******/ var getProto = Object.getPrototypeOf ? (obj) => (Object.getPrototypeOf(obj)) : (obj) => (obj.__proto__); /******/ var leafPrototypes; /******/ // create a fake namespace object /******/ // mode & 1: value is a module id, require it /******/ // mode & 2: merge all properties of value into the ns /******/ // mode & 4: return value when already ns object /******/ // mode & 16: return value when it's Promise-like /******/ // mode & 8|1: behave like require /******/ __webpack_require__.t = function(value, mode) { /******/ if(mode & 1) value = this(value); /******/ if(mode & 8) return value; /******/ if(typeof value === 'object' && value) { /******/ if((mode & 4) && value.__esModule) return value; /******/ if((mode & 16) && typeof value.then === 'function') return value; /******/ } /******/ var ns = Object.create(null); /******/ __webpack_require__.r(ns); /******/ var def = {}; /******/ leafPrototypes = leafPrototypes || [null, getProto({}), getProto([]), getProto(getProto)]; /******/ for(var current = mode & 2 && value; typeof current == 'object' && !~leafPrototypes.indexOf(current); current = getProto(current)) { /******/ Object.getOwnPropertyNames(current).forEach((key) => (def[key] = () => (value[key]))); /******/ } /******/ def['default'] = () => (value); /******/ __webpack_require__.d(ns, def); console.log('enter'); /******/ return ns; /******/ }; /******/ })(); ```

because `stream_browserify` is a function here, it can't match the condition(`typeof value === 'object'`) in `__webpack_require__.t`, so the `stream_browserify_namespaceObject` only has the default property. Is this a bad case in `__webpack_require__.t`? And what's the difference between `t` and `n`, it seems both of them handle namespace import **If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.** I try to reproduce it in a simply webpack project, but it use `.n`, so it works fine there, I haven't figured out how to create the same output **What is the expected behavior?** get full property of namespace object **Other relevant information:** webpack version: 5.8.22 Node.js version: 20.16.0 Operating System: Mac OS Additional tools:
author: HomyeeKing

title: Webpack filesystem cache questions
body:
Hi Webpack team 👋 , 

I have some questions regarding the use of the filesystem cache feature.
For context, these questions relate to adding filesystem caching to our production builds. Thank you for taking the time to review them!

1. In the default config setting (`config: [__filename]`), are transitive dependencies from our codebase included along the entire dependency tree? For example, does this include imports of the imports used in the Webpack configuration file? (via `require()`)
2. How are loaders and plugins included in the cache? How does invalidation work?
If we use custom loaders that are referenced dynamically (e.g., via `path.resolve`), should we explicitly track these dependencies, along with any other dynamic imports? For example:
`loader: path.resolve(baseDirPath, 'webpack/loaders/json-loader.js'),`
3. Should build orchestration scripts that invoke the Webpack CLI be included as build dependencies? I believe [these docs](https://github.com/webpack/changelog-v5/blob/master/guides/persistent-caching.md) suggest they should, but I wanted to confirm.
4. Would you recommend adding `package.json` & `yarn.lock` as a dependency, as a way to track npm dependency changes? For example like so:
```
dependencies: [
    path.resolve(baseDirPath, 'package.json'),
    path.resolve(baseDirPath, 'yarn.lock'),
]
```
5. Webpack CLI arguments and environment parameters passed via the CLI would invalidate the cache if their values change, correct?
6. Do you have any recommended strategies for optimizing cache generation in a multi-environment setup (e.g., dev, QA, prod)? Specifically, are there best practices for cache clearing in CI pipelines, setting an age limit, and how hashes compare to timestamps? (we've currently configured `hash: true` for CI)
7. Is there a way to implement partial cache invalidation for files that we know will be rewritten (e.g., HTML generated by `HtmlWebpackPlugin`), rather than invalidating an entire cache pack?

title: Inconsistent Module Resolution (context require vs. ES6 import)
body:
<!-- Please don't delete this template because we'll close your issue -->
<!-- Before creating an issue please make sure you are using the latest version of webpack. -->

# Bug report

<!-- Please ask questions on StackOverflow or the GitHub Discussions. -->
<!-- https://github.com/webpack/webpack/discussions -->
<!-- https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=webpack -->
<!-- Issues which contain questions or support requests will be closed. -->

**What is the current behavior?**
webpack treats relative context required files as modules when it match the `module` configuration , which is inconsistent with import statement

```javascript
// file structure
// ./src/mods
//      ./1.js
//      ./2.js
// index.js

// configuration
resolve.modules: ['node_modules', path.resolve(__dirname, 'src')],

// index.js
const req = require.context('./mods', true, /\.js$/); // both 1.js and 2.js are modules
import mod1Relative from './mods/1.js'; // not a module
import mod1Alias from 'mods/2.js'; // module
```

**how I declar the 'module'**:
use rawModule hook
```javascript
resolver.getHook('before-rawModule').tapAsync('runtime-external-resolve', (req, ctx, cb) => {
      console.log('----isModule: ', req.request);
      return cb();
  });
```

**If the current behavior is a bug, please provide the steps to reproduce.**
https://github.com/suguanYang/webpack-context-require-issue

keep the same behavior as the import statement, a relative path should take precedence

    test: /shared-workers[\\/][^\\/]+[\\/]esm2015[\\/][^\\/]+\.script\.ts$/,

    exclude: /node_modules/,
    generator: {
        filename: 'shared-workers/[name].script.js' //

But I cannot access it when the Dev Server is running.
It should be located on `http://localhost/shared-workers/worker.script.js`
